summary: Welcome to BetterLSAT!
text_content:
Welcome to BetterLSAT!

I’m Mustafa, the founder of BetterLSAT. Unfortunately, today most LSAT prep feels like it was designed in the 1990s—dry, overly academic, and unnecessarily complicated. I decided to make a change. You’re looking for the best way to master this test, and you’ve found it.

At BetterLSAT, we don't just teach the test; we decode it. We’ve helped hundreds of students—from those just starting at a 140 to those chasing a 175—shatter their plateaus and secure seats at the most prestigious law schools in North America. We didn't get them there by making them memorize dusty logic textbooks. We cut the nonsense and focus exclusively on the patterns that get you points. It’s about working smarter, not longer.

Our course is the culmination of everything we’ve learned helping students reach the 99th percentile. We’re here to turn you into a scoring machine so you can stop worrying about the LSAT and start focusing on which law school hoodie you want to buy.

Let’s get started.

summary: To start off, I want to be real with you.
text_content:
To start off, I want to be real with you.

The LSAT is a marathon. I’ll probably say that a dozen more times before we’re done, not to scare you, but to make sure you’re mentally prepared for the distance. You aren't just sitting for a test; you're training for a high-intensity endurance event.

Next, if anyone tells you that you can master this test in a weekend, they’re lying. This test requires stamina, consistency, and a mental toughness that most other standardized tests don't ask for.

The LSAT is challenging for two specific reasons:

Pattern Complexity. It tests a very specific type of logic and analytical reasoning that most students have never seen in a classroom.

The Clock. You have roughly 1 minute and 24 seconds per question to perform complex logical gymnastics.

So, how do we handle a marathon that’s also a sprint?

While other courses drown you in analogies, we use a two-phase approach to ensure you don't burn out by mile ten:

Phase 1: Precision (The Foundation): We master the core language of the test first. We get you to the point where you can see the right answer before you even look at the choices. Speed is irrelevant if you're heading in the wrong direction.

Phase 2: Ramping (The Sprint): Once your accuracy is bulletproof, we layer on the pressure. We teach you how to maintain that precision while the clock is ticking.

The journey is long, but the shortcut is real. We’ve decoded the patterns so you can focus on the reps that actually move the needle.

You bring the effort; we’ll provide the map.

Recap: The LSAT is a high-intensity endurance event that tests both complex logic and extreme speed. To survive this marathon, we use Optimized Execution: focusing on Phase 1 (Precision) to master the language first, followed by Phase 2 (Ramping) to maintain that accuracy under the pressure of the 84-second clock.

summary: Before we get lost in the "minutiae" of logic—debating whether "most" includes "all" or if "some" can mean "just one"—we need to take a massive step back. You are about to spend dozens, perhaps hundreds, of hours in the lab. You deserve to know exactly why this test is worth that
text_content:
Before we get lost in the "minutiae" of logic—debating whether "most" includes "all" or if "some" can mean "just one"—we need to take a massive step back. You are about to spend dozens, perhaps hundreds, of hours in the lab. You deserve to know exactly why this test is worth that investment.

The short answer? It’s the single most powerful lever in your professional life.

The 50/70 Rule.

Think about your undergraduate career. You spent four years, dozens of sleepless nights, and thousands of dollars to build a GPA. In the eyes of an admissions committee at a school like Yale and UVA, that four-year effort is often given the same weight as—or even less weight than—a single three-hour test.

In most law school admissions formulas, the LSAT accounts for 50% to 70% of your entry profile. That is a staggering reality. It means that every hour you spend in this course is worth more than a month of your undergraduate studies.

The Highest Hourly Wage You’ll Ever Earn

Most people see the LSAT as a barrier. I want you to see it as a paycheck.

Law schools use the LSAT to hand out merit-based scholarships. A 3-point jump in your score isn't just about getting into a better school; it’s often the difference between paying $150,000 in tuition and getting a full-ride scholarship. If you study for 100 hours and secure an extra $50,000 in funding, you just earned $500 per hour. Most test takers will fail to understand this, the others give priority to this test to save boatloads of money later.

There is no other job a student can get that pays that well.

The "Median" Game: How Schools Think

To beat the admissions officers, you have to understand the game they are playing. Every Law School Dean is obsessed with one thing: U.S. News Rankings (or their Canadian equivalents). These rankings are heavily influenced by the median LSAT score of the incoming class.

Notice I said median, not average. This is a crucial distinction.

Imagine a law school line of 200 students. The admissions officer only cares about the person standing at number 100. If that person has a 171, the school’s ranking stays high. If they have a 170, the school drops, the alumni get angry, and the Dean has a very bad year.

This is your opportunity. If a school's median is a 165 and you have a 173, you are "Gold." You are helping them protect their ranking. Because of this "Median War," schools will often overlook a lower GPA if your LSAT score helps pull their median up. This is how you "hack" your way into a school that might otherwise be out of reach, or negotiate for money you otherwise wouldn’t have.

The Four Strategic Tiers

Admissions offices generally categorize you into one of four "buckets" based on their specific medians:

The Safe Bet (Double Above): You are above their LSAT median and their GPA median. You aren't just getting in; you’re fighting for a scholarship.

The Splitter (LSAT Heavy): Your GPA is below their median, but your LSAT is above. You are a "rankings booster." They need you to balance out the students with high GPAs but low scores.

The Reverse Splitter (GPA Heavy): Your GPA is high, but your LSAT is a bit low. You are "GPA insurance" for them, but you’re a riskier admit.

The Long Shot (Double Below): You are below both medians. To get in here, your "story"—your work experience, your personal statement, and your "why"—has to be undeniable.

The Human Element

Numbers get your file off the "maybe" pile and onto the "serious consideration" desk. Once you are in that "serious" pile, the conversation shifts. Admissions officers stop looking at your spreadsheet and start looking at you.

They ask: Is this person actually going to be a good lawyer? Are they resilient? Do they have a perspective that will enrich our 1L classrooms?

But remember: you never get to have that "human" conversation if your numbers don't put you in the right bucket first. That is why we are here. We aren't just trying to get you a "good" score; we are trying to put you in a position where the law school is chasing you, not the other way around.

Always remember: this test is the gatekeeper, but it’s also your ticket to the front of the line.

Recap: This test is the single biggest lever in your professional life, often weighing more than your entire four-year GPA. By understanding the "Median Game" and how schools use your score to protect their rankings, you can move from a "Long Shot" to a "Safe Bet" and potentially earn the highest hourly wage of your life through merit-based scholarships.
source_heading: Module 1, Lesson 3: Why this Test is Crucial

course_description: Core BetterLSAT syllabus imported from DOCX
summary: If you spend more than five minutes on a law school forum, you’ll run into a mountain of bad advice. It’s important we clear out any mental clutter. Most people view this test as a mysterious, unbeatable monster, but it's really just a game with a specific set of rules.
text_content:
If you spend more than five minutes on a law school forum, you’ll run into a mountain of bad advice. It’s important we clear out any mental clutter. Most people view this test as a mysterious, unbeatable monster, but it's really just a game with a specific set of rules.

Here are the five biggest myths we need to debunk before you move forward.

Myth #1: The LSAT is an IQ Test

The Truth: This is the most dangerous myth because it suggests your score is "fixed" from birth. If you think this test measures innate intelligence, you’ll treat a low practice score as a personal ceiling rather than a starting point. It isn’t an IQ test; it is a test of proficiency in a specific language. Just like you weren't born knowing how to speak a second language or write code, you aren't born knowing how to deconstruct formal logic and arguments. It is a learned skill that requires a foreign way of processing information. At BetterLSAT, we see score jumps every day because we treat logic as a muscle that can be trained through disciplined "Rep Work," not a static measure of how smart you are. Once you learn the language of the test, the mystery disappears.

Myth #2: My high GPA will carry me

The Truth: A 4.0 is a fantastic achievement that shows years of discipline, but it doesn’t give you a "pass" on the LSAT. Law schools know that GPAs are not created equal; they vary wildly across different majors, universities, and even specific professors.

The LSAT serves as the Universal Equalizer. It is the one metric that allows an admissions committee at a law school to compare a business major from a low-tier school to a chemistry major from an Ivy League school on the exact same scale. While your GPA reflects four years of effort, your LSAT score often accounts for 50% to 70% of your admissions profile. Even with a perfect GPA, your LSAT score is what determines which bucket your application falls into and how much scholarship money is on the table.

Myth #3: The test has nothing to do with practicing law

The Truth: A common trope: "I'll never use this in the real world." While it’s true you’ll probably never have to diagram a conditional statement in a courtroom, you will have to read a 60-page judicial opinion and find the tiny logical flaw that wins your client’s case.

The LSAT tests structural reading, attention to detail, and the ability to spot weak arguments in a short time frame. These are the core mechanics of a lawyer's daily life. Every time you analyze a stimulus in our lab, you are practicing the exact mental gymnastics required to thrive in 1L and beyond. We aren't just teaching you how to pass a test; we are teaching you the "Lawyer Mindset" of identifying flaws and alternative explanations.

Myth #4: You should only take the LSAT once

The Truth: The "one-and-done" mentality is a relic of an older admissions era. Today, almost every law school in North America only cares about your highest score. Admissions officers don't see a retake as a sign of weakness; they see a student who is committed to excellence and willing to put in the work to improve.

LSAC allows you to take the test up to five times in the current testing cycle and seven times in your life. If you don’t hit your target on the first try, you shouldn't panic or cancel. Instead, you go back to the lab, use our Analytics platform to find your weaknesses, and go again. Your highest score is the only one that helps their medians, so it’s the only one that truly matters to them.

Myth #5: You can’t significantly improve your score

The Truth: This is the myth we debunk most often. Your diagnostic (starting) score is simply a measure of how much you don't know about the test's patterns yet. It is not a prediction of your potential.

We’ve seen students start in the 140s and work their way into the 160s and 170s by replacing "guessing" with a repeatable, pattern-based system. I personally reached a 176 by treating the test as a set of predictable logic patterns to be decoded rather than a series of random questions. If you are willing to move away from "academic bloat" and focus on the reps that actually move the needle, your score will follow.

Now that the air is clear, it’s time to look at the actual test content. Let's move on to Lesson 5: The New LSAT Landscape.

Recap: We’ve cleared the mental fog by debunking the idea that the LSAT is an IQ test—it’s actually a learnable language that anyone can master with the right strategy. Remember: your high score is the only one that truly matters to admissions, and your starting diagnostic is a measure of what you haven't learned yet, not your potential.
source_heading: Module 1, Lesson 4: The LSAT: Myths vs. Reality
module_label: Module 1: , Lesson 4: The LSAT: Myths vs. Reality

summary: Now that we’ve cleared the air on the myths, it’s time to look at the actual blueprint of the test you’re about to master. The LSAT has changed significantly in recent years—most notably with the removal of the “Logic Games” section—so it is vital that you understand the current
text_content:
Now that we’ve cleared the air on the myths, it’s time to look at the actual blueprint of the test you’re about to master. The LSAT has changed significantly in recent years—most notably with the removal of the “Logic Games” section—so it is vital that you understand the current landscape before you start your first practice test.

The Big Picture: LSAC and the Move to In-Person

The LSAT is administered by the Law School Admission Council (LSAC). For nearly every law school in North America, this test is the primary predictor of your first-year success and a mandatory part of your application.

Currently, the LSAC is undergoing a major shift in how the test is delivered. While we have enjoyed a mix of remote and in-person options for the last few years, starting in August 2026, the LSAT is moving almost entirely to in-person testing at digital centers. Except for specific medical accommodations or extreme geographic hardship, you should expect to take your exam in a professional proctored environment. Regardless of the setting, the test is purely digital; you’ll use a computer interface to view questions, though scratch paper and pencils will still be provided for rough work or diagramming.

The Players: Logical Reasoning vs. Reading Comp

Before we break down the timing, you need to understand the two core disciplines that now dominate the exam.

Logical Reasoning (LR): This is the heart of the modern LSAT. It consists of short "mini-arguments" or stimuli, usually 2 to 5 sentences long. In a standard section, you’ll face roughly 24 to 26 questions, typically with one question per passage.Note for the Lab: In older practice materials (PrepTests), you might occasionally see two questions tied to one stimulus. While the modern exam has moved away from this, the underlying logic remains identical.

Reading Comprehension (RC): This is the ultimate test of structural reading and mental stamina. Instead of short bursts, you are given four long passages, each approximately 400–600 words. Each passage is followed by a set of 5 to 8 questions. Success here isn’t just about knowing what the author said, but why they said it and how they built their case or argument.

The Section Breakdown: The "3+1" Format

On test day, you will sit through four sections, each lasting 35 minutes. However, only three of these sections are "scored" and contribute to your 120–180 result.

The scored math for the 2026 cycle is as follows:

2 Scored Sections of Logical Reasoning (LR).

1 Scored Section of Reading Comprehension (RC).

The fourth section is the Unscored (Experimental) Section. LSAC uses this to vet new material for future exams. The "twist" is that you won’t know which section is the experimental one while the clock is ticking. This leads to two possible test-day scenarios:

3 LR / 1 RC: One of your three LR sections is "fake".

2 LR / 2 RC: One of your two RC sections is unscored.

Because there is no way to identify the unscored section, you must treat every single question as if it determines your final score.

The Workflow: Intermission and Writing

The LSAT is as much a test of endurance as it is of logic.

The Break: You will complete the first two sections back-to-back, followed by a mandatory 10-minute intermission. During this time, you can stretch, eat a quick snack, or use the restroom. After the break, you will dive into the final two sections.

LSAT Argumentative Writing: This is a separate, unscored section that is administered remotely for everyone. While it doesn't affect your numerical score, you must have a completed writing sample on file to see your LSAT results. We recommend completing this within 8 days after your multiple-choice exam to avoid any delays in your score release.

The Scoring: Raw vs. Scaled

Understanding the "math" of your score is essential for strategic planning.

Raw Score: This is the total number of questions you answered correctly across your three scored sections—usually out of 75 to 78 total questions. Crucially, there is no penalty for guessing. If you are running out of time, you should always fill in every bubble; a 20% chance of a "blind guess" is better than a 0% chance of an empty one.

Scaled Score (120–180): Your raw score is converted into a scaled score using a statistical process called equating.

The "Curve": It is a common misconception that you are curved against the other people in the room. In reality, the scale is set based on the inherent difficulty of the questions. If you get a particularly brutal test, the scale will be more "lenient"—for example, you might miss 11 questions and still score a 170. On an easier test, that same 170 might only allow for 8 misses.

Accommodations: Leveling the Playing Field

At BetterLSAT, we believe in maximizing every resource available to you. Through their commitment to disability rights, LSAC provides a wide range of testing accommodations for students with documented needs.

Available Support: This can include extra time (often 50% or 100% more per section), additional or longer breaks, a paper-and-pencil format, or the use of specific medical devices.

Automatic Approval: If you have been previously approved for LSAT accommodations, you will typically be automatically approved for future attempts.

The Process: You must submit your request via JD Services by the same deadline as your test registration. Documentation from a medical professional is usually required, so start this process as soon as you register.

Personal Items: Note that some items, like non-tinted eyeglasses, medication, and certain medical monitors, are allowed for all test takers without prior approval.

Recap: The modern LSAT is a digital, four-section test focused exclusively on Logical Reasoning and Reading Comprehension. With two scored LR sections, one scored RC, and a hidden experimental section, your strategy must be to treat every question as if it determines your future.
source_heading: Module 1, Lesson 5: The New LSAT Landscape
module_label: Module 1: , Lesson 5: The New LSAT Landscape

summary: To beat the test, you have to speak the language. Law school admissions officers and the LSAC love their technical jargon, and if you want to navigate the BetterLSAT lab like a pro, you need to know exactly what we’re referring to when we break down a problem.
text_content:
To beat the test, you have to speak the language. Law school admissions officers and the LSAC love their technical jargon, and if you want to navigate the BetterLSAT lab like a pro, you need to know exactly what we’re referring to when we break down a problem.

Every Logical Reasoning question is built using the same three components. If you ignore one, the whole thing falls apart.

1. The Stimulus

In the world of Logical Reasoning, the Stimulus is the short paragraph at the top of the question. It’s usually between two and five sentences long, and it’s the "raw material" you have to work with.

This is where the test-makers set the stage. Sometimes they’ll give you a coherent argument with a premise and a conclusion; other times, they’ll just give you a random pile of facts that don't seem to lead anywhere. Your job is to dissect this passage with surgical precision.

Insert Pic: Example of an LR Stimulus

2. The Question Stem

The Question Stem is the actual instruction—the part that tells you what your "mission" is. It sits right between the stimulus and the answer choices.

This is where the LSAC starts their psychological warfare. They might ask you to find a flaw, strengthen a claim, or identify an assumption. If you misread a single word in the stem, you could be answering a completely different question than the one they asked. We’ve seen students do brilliant work on the stimulus, only to fail because they "strengthened" an argument when the stem specifically asked them to "weaken" it.

Insert Pic: Example of a Question Stem

3. The Answer Choices

Finally, you have the Answer Choices. You’ll always be given five options (A through E).

This is the minefield. In every question, the LSAC spends about 10% of their time writing the correct answer and 90% of their time crafting "sucker" choices designed to gaslight you. These are the choices that play on your assumptions, use "strong" language to scare you off, or repeat words from the stimulus to trick your brain into thinking they’re relevant.

Our goal in the lab is to get you so good at the logic that you can predict the right answer before you even look at these options. When you know the patterns, the four "wrong" choices start to look like obvious traps rather than legitimate possibilities.

Insert Pic: Example of Answer Choices A-E

Now that you know the anatomy, it’s time to tackle a real question.

Recap:

The Stimulus: The "raw material" or passage you need to analyze.

The Question Stem: Your specific mission or set of instructions.

The Answer Choices: One gold nugget surrounded by four very expensive-looking traps.

Module 1, ACTIVE DRILL Musical Performances

Encore! Encore! Did you ever get dragged to a cousin’s garage band performance and realize within thirty seconds that you’d rather be doing literally anything else? Well, this question isn't about that. It’s about "inspired" music. But don't let the high-brow talk about sophisticated listeners distract you, this is just a logic puzzle dressed up in a tuxedo.

But if the thought process you just went through feels slow and mentally draining, that’s because it is.

Here is the secret: Top scorers in the lab don’t spend ten minutes puzzling through the meaning of a sophisticated listener. They don’t care about the music at all. They ignore the content and look at the skeleton of the question.

Almost all LSAT questions are "cookie-cutter." The LSAC has a set of reasoning patterns they love to test, and they simply bake dozens of different questions using the exact same mold. Through a deep understanding of these patterns, you can start zeroing in on the correct answer with less mental energy.

The "Cookie-Cutter" Logic

The pattern involved in this question is a Logic Chain. It looks like this:

All A are B.

All B are C.

All C are D.

Thus, if you have A, you must have D.

It doesn't make intuitive sense yet? That’s okay. Let’s look at a few other "cookies" cut from this exact same mold:

If you buy a King-sized bed, it is Comfy.

If it is Comfy, you get Great Sleep.

If you get Great Sleep, you are Energized Waking Up.

What else must be true? If you buy a King-sized bed, you are energized waking up

What about this one?

Every Great Manager creates Efficient Ops.

Every Efficient Op leads to Higher Profit.

Higher Profit means Tax Evasion

What else must be true? Every Great Manager leads to Tax Evasion

Last one.

If there’s an Inspired Musical Performance, there’s a Good Show.

If there’s a Good Show, there are Sophisticated Listeners.

If there are Sophisticated Listeners, they Understand Musical Roots

See how we went about it? The "Inspired Music" question is just a cookie cut from that same mold. If you want a deeper dive into how we translate into diagrams, review this lesson.

When we link them together, we get one long, beautiful chain:

Inspired Performance → Good Show → Sophisticated Listeners → Understand Roots

The "Mirror" Chain (The Contrapositive)

If the chain works forward, it also works in reverse if you flip it and make it negative. We call this the contrapositive. If the dominoes didn't fall, the first one couldn't have been tipped.

If you didn't get a Great Sleep, you definitely weren't in that Comfy, King-sized bed.

If you didn’t do Tax Evasion, there are no Great Managers.

You get the point.

Our Chain (Contrapositive):

Don't Understand Roots → No Sophisticated Listeners → No Good Show → No Inspired Performance

We must keep this chain in mind when going into the answer choices as well.

Answer Choice A:

Choice (A) says: "If there are no sophisticated listeners... then there will be no inspired performances."

If we look back at our contrapositive chain:

Don't Understand Roots → No Sophisticated Listeners → No Good Show → No Inspired Performance

It’s a perfect match with our chain. If you take away the "Sophisticated Listeners," there is no way there can be an inspired musical performance.

Answer Choice B:

Choice (B) says: "If there isn’t a good show... then there will be no one who understands their musical roots"

This answer choice goes in the other direction from our arrows in our contrapositive chain:

Don't Understand Roots → No Sophisticated Listeners → No Good Show → No Inspired Performance

Answer Choice C:

Choice (C) says: "If there are people who understand musical roots.. then there will be an inspired performance"

This answer choice goes in the other direction from our arrows in our regular chain:

Inspired Performance → Good Show → Sophisticated Listeners → Understand Roots

While an inspired performance requires roots, having roots doesn't guarantee the performance is inspired. You can understand your musical roots perfectly and still give a boring, uninspired performance.

Answer Choice D:

Choice (D) says: "If there are people who understand musical roots.. then there will be a good show"

To understand how we translated this, you can refer to the lesson here.

Again, this answer choice goes in the other direction from our arrows in our regular chain:

Inspired Performance → Good Show → Sophisticated Listeners → Understand Roots

The stimulus says a Good Show requires roots. Choice (D) flips it and claims that having roots is enough to guarantee a good show. Again, just because you have the "requirement" (roots) doesn't mean the "result" (good show) automatically happens. You're trying to force the dominoes to fall in the other direction.

Answer Choice E:

Lastly, Choice (E) says: "If there are sophisticated listeners.. then there will be an inspired performance"

Again, this answer choice goes in the other direction from our arrows in our regular chain:

Inspired Performance → Good Show → Sophisticated Listeners → Understand Roots

The stimulus says you need the listeners to have the performance. It does not say that whenever you have listeners, a performance magically becomes inspired. There could be a room full of sophisticated listeners sitting in silence or listening to someone play the recorder poorly.

See that?

At first glance, this question looks mind-boggling. The answer choices are a word-salad of "sophisticated listeners" and "musical roots" that all seem to be saying the exact same thing. Most students get caught in that loop, reading the same sentence five times until their brain shorts out.

But, even when a question looks intimidating, it’s usually just a complex mask over a very simple structure. Once you can strip away the superficial fluff and see the pattern underneath, those identical-looking answer choices suddenly start to look very different. You’ll see the traps for what they are, and the correct answer will practically jump off the screen.

It’s important to stop being a "reader" and start being a strategist. Learn these patterns, and you’ll be zeroing in on 170+ scores with the confidence of someone who already knows how the game is rigged.

Be prepared to put in the reps and score in the top 1%.

Welcome to BetterLSAT.

Recap: We took a dense, confusing paragraph and proved that it was actually just a simple logic chain in a tuxedo. By learning to "Follow the Arrow" and identify the Contrapositive, we saw how to eliminate choices and find the one answer that must be true. We’ve shifted from passive reading to active structural analysis—the primary skill of every top-scoring law student.
source_heading: Module 1, Lesson 6: The Lexicon: Speaking "LSAT"
module_label: Module 1: , Lesson 6: The Lexicon: Speaking "LSAT"

summary: So, orientation is officially over.
text_content:
So, orientation is officially over.

By now, you’ve seen behind the curtain. You know why this test matters, you’ve debunked the myths that hold most students back, and you’ve even decoded a high-level logic chain that makes most people's heads spin. You are no longer a "stressed applicant"; you are a strategist with a map.

But knowing the rules of the arena is different than winning the fight.

Moving to Execution

Everything we have done so far has been about the "What" and the "Why." Starting with the next module, we move into the "How." We are going to stop talking about the test and start dismantling it, one argument at a time.

In Module 2: Arguments, we dive into the DNA of the LSAT. Everything on this test is built on the same foundation: The Argument. We are going to teach you how to:

Identify the "conclusion" with total certainty (so you stop falling for traps).

Spot the "assumptions" that the test-makers are hiding in plain sight.

Understand the "spectrum of support" that determines whether an argument is bulletproof or garbage.

The Lab Mindset

As you move forward, remember the BetterLSAT promise: no academic bloat. Every lesson in the next module is an eventual tool designed to be used under the clock. You have the basics down—now it’s time to use it.

You’re ready to move from the sidelines into the trenches. The work gets harder from here, but this is where the 170+ is won.

See you in Module 2.

Recap: You’ve successfully navigated the Kickoff module and now have a complete understanding of the LSAT’s landscape, scoring, and fundamental jargon. We are now transitioning from orientation to active execution, where we will begin mastering Arguments—the core building block of every point you will earn in the lab.

The Anatomy of an Argument

Module 2: “The Anatomy of an Argument”

The “7Sage LSAT” Format:

Let’s look at the way 7Sage has categorized their arguments module in Foundations:

Our Goal: A comprehensive "instruction-then-exercise" flow that makes the 7Sage module effective, but we’re stripping away the repetitive "philosophy" lessons (like why assumptions are "subtle") to keep the momentum high and push that we don’t waste time in stuff that doesn’t bring results.

***Our modules need to look and have the same features as 7Sage

OUR “The Anatomy of an Argument” Module Lesson Breakdown:

Estimated Time: ~6-7 Hours

Goal: Learn to deconstruct any stimulus into its logical structure.

Below is the breakdown of how our “The Anatomy of an Argument” will be structured instead, emulating the same format but changing the titles respectively and making it unique to BetterLSAT:

Lesson Title

Format

Description

The Logic of Connection

Text

Intro: Establishing that every argument is just a relationship between a claim and its proof.

The Foundation: Premises & Conclusions

Text

Defining the two "must-have" components of any argument.

The GPS: Indicator Words

Text

Mastering the "Conclusion" and "Premise" signposts (Thus, Therefore, Since, Because).

The "Support" System

Text

How to tell if a sentence is doing the "proving" or being "proven."

Rep Work: Following the Signs

Rep Work

Exercise: Categorizing words into "Conclusion-starters" vs. "Premise-starters."

Rep Work: Identifying Premises and Conclusions (Easy)

Rep Work

Exercise: 10 short paragraphs where students must highlight the Conclusion.

summary: If you want to scale a business, you have to understand the relationship between supply and demand. If you want to scale your LSAT score, you have to understand the relationship between ideas.
text_content:
If you want to scale a business, you have to understand the relationship between supply and demand. If you want to scale your LSAT score, you have to understand the relationship between ideas.

At its core, the LSAT is not a test of "facts." It doesn’t care if you know anything about prehistoric fish, judicial activism, or the mating habits of polar bears. It cares about how those ideas connect. In the lab, we don't get distracted by the "content"; we focus exclusively on what holds those ideas together.

What is a Relationship?

A relationship is the abstract link between two or more ideas. In logic, we call the ideas themselves "components," and the connection between them is the "relationship."

Think of it like a bridge. One side of the bridge is Point A, the other side is Point B, and the bridge itself is the Relationship. You can’t have a bridge without the two pieces of land it connects, just as you can’t have a relationship without at least two ideas to give it meaning.

The LSAT "Menu" of Connections

While the "content" of the test changes every time, the types of relationships stay the same. You are going to learn to spot these "cookie-cutter" connections instantly:

Support Relationships: One idea acts as the "legs" that hold up another idea (Premise → Conclusion).

Comparative Relationships: One idea is "faster," "cheaper," or "more efficient" than another.

Causal Relationships: One event is responsible for making another event happen (Cause → Effect).

Conditional Relationships: One idea acting as a "trigger" that guarantees another idea (Sufficient → Necessary).

By shifting your focus from the what to the how, you stop being a passive reader and start being a logic architect. You’ll begin to see that every stimulus is just a set of points connected by invisible lines. Your job in this module is to make those lines visible.

Recap: We’ve established that a huge component of the LSAT is a test of relationships, not specific facts. A relationship is simply the "bridge" between two ideas, and mastering these connections is the secret to cutting through the academic bloat of the test to find the correct answer quickly.
source_heading: Module 2, Lesson 1: The Logic of Connection
module_label: Module 2: , Lesson 1: The Logic of Connection

summary: In the world of logic, a relationship is simply an abstract connection between two or more components. While there are many types of connections on this test—comparative, causal, or conditional—the single most important relationship you will ever encounter is the Support Relation
text_content:
In the world of logic, a relationship is simply an abstract connection between two or more components. While there are many types of connections on this test—comparative, causal, or conditional—the single most important relationship you will ever encounter is the Support Relationship.

This relationship is the "DNA" of an argument. If you can’t accurately identify which parts are doing the supporting and which part is being supported, you aren't actually analyzing the test—you’re just reading it.

The Anatomy of an Argument

On the LSAT, an argument isn't a disagreement; it is a structural attempt to prove a point. Every argument is composed of at least two parts:

The Conclusion: A claim that is supported by another claim (The Premise)

The Premise: A claim that supports another claim (The Conclusion)

1. The Conclusion (The Destination)

The conclusion is the most important part of the stimulus. It is the claim the author is fighting for—the primary point they want you to walk away believing.

Think of the conclusion as the "Point of the Spear." Everything else in the paragraph is designed to push that point forward.

It is a Claim: Conclusions are often opinions, recommendations, or predictions.

It is Vulnerable: Because a conclusion is a claim, it requires proof. It cannot stand on its own.

The Position Trap: A conclusion can appear anywhere—at the beginning, in the middle, or at the end. Top scorers never assume a sentence is a conclusion just because of where it sits.

2. The Premise (The Building Blocks)

Premises are the supporting facts. These are the reasons why we should accept the conclusion.

In the BetterLSAT lab, we treat premises as accepted facts. You don't get points for arguing with the premises. If the author says, "Most startups fail in the first year," you accept that as a truth for the duration of that question. Your job is to see if those facts actually lead to the conclusion the author is trying to reach.

They are Evidence: They provide the "Support" for the claim.

They are the Foundation: Without premises, a conclusion cannot exist.

The Support Relationship in Action

Support is the relationship that connects the premises to the conclusion.

Example Argument #1:

Since high interest rates usually decrease consumer spending, the central bank’s recent rate hike will likely lead to a cooling economy.

The Premise (The Fact): High interest rates usually decrease consumer spending.

The Conclusion (The Prediction): The rate hike will likely lead to a cooling economy.

Example Argument #2:

Because the office lease is doubling in price next month, we should transition the entire team to a fully remote work model.

Premise (The "Why"): The office lease is doubling in price next month.

Conclusion (The "Ask"): We should transition the entire team to a fully remote work model.

Example Argument #3:

Investing in the new AI startup is a reckless move for the firm, given that the lead developer resigned last week without a replacement and that we don’t have the money for it

Premise #1 (The "Why"): The lead developer resigned last week without a replacement.

Premise #2 (The "Why"): We don’t have the money for it.

Conclusion (The "Ask"): Investing in the new AI startup is a reckless move for the firm.

It is important to know there is one and only one main conclusion of every argument, but there can be multiple premises. Whatever it may be, Order Doesn’t Matter.

Deciphering the Argument

Now that you know what an argument is, you might be asking: How do I actually find these pieces in a dense, 60-word paragraph? There are two main ways to decipher which part is the evidence and which is the goal, and we’re going to master them in the next few lessons.

Recap: Every argument is a structural relationship where Premises (the evidence) are used to justify a Conclusion (the main claim). To master this, you must treat premises as facts and focus your energy on whether they provide enough support to reach the "Goal" of the argument.

summary: Sometimes, the LSAC actually decides to be helpful. In many stimuli, they leave behind "bread crumbs" that tell you exactly where the conclusion is and which sentences are there to support it. In the lab, we call these Indicator Words. This is the first (easier) tool you can use
text_content:
Sometimes, the LSAC actually decides to be helpful. In many stimuli, they leave behind "bread crumbs" that tell you exactly where the conclusion is and which sentences are there to support it. In the lab, we call these Indicator Words. This is the first (easier) tool you can use to understand which way the support flows.

Think of these as the GPS for an argument. If you know how to read the signs, you don't have to guess where you’re going; the test-makers are literally flagging their moves for you.

1. Conclusion Indicators (The "Goal" Flags)

When you see these words, the author is telling you: "Attention! This is the point I’m trying to make." The statement following these words is almost always the conclusion.

Standard Flags:

Thus

Therefore

In conclusion

Hence

It follows that

Shows

Consequently

As a result

So

Accordingly

Clearly

The "Pivot" Flags:

However

But

Yet

Strategist's Secret: "However" and "But" are special. When an author spends the first two sentences telling you what other people think (context), they use a pivot word to "turn" the argument toward their own view. That turn is almost always where the conclusion lives.

Example: "Some analysts argue the market will crash. But the current data suggests we are headed for a bull run." (The conclusion is that we are headed for a bull run).

2. Premise Indicators (The "Evidence" Flags)

These words are the signals for the "Why." They introduce the facts, data, or reasons that support the conclusion.

Standard Flags:

Because

For example

For

Since

Given that

Due to

For this reason

After all

Special Premise Indicators

There are five special premise indicators that do more than just mark a single sentence. These include: Moreover, Furthermore, In Addition, Also, and Likewise.

When you see these, they aren't just telling you the current sentence is a premise—they are acting like logical glue. They tell you that both the claim following it AND the claim immediately before it are premises working together to support the goal.

Example 1: The Standard Flow

The marketing campaign resulted in a 20% increase in lead generation. Consequently, the campaign should be considered a success.

Conclusion: The campaign should be considered a success.

Premise: The campaign resulted in a 20% increase in lead generation.

Justification: This uses a classic "Conclusion Indicator" (Consequently) to bridge the gap between a raw data point and a value judgment.

Example 2: Conclusion First

The firm must hire more analysts, since the current workload is unsustainable for the existing team.

Conclusion: The firm must hire more analysts.

Premise: The current workload is unsustainable for the existing team.

Justification: This is a "Conclusion First" structure. The word since acts as a Premise Indicator, flagging the second half of the sentence as the "Why" that justifies the "Ask" at the beginning.

Example 3: The Additive Chain

The new software is encrypted with 256-bit security. Moreover, it requires multi-factor authentication for all users. Therefore, our data is safe.

Conclusion: Our data is safe.

Premise 1: The software is encrypted with 256-bit security.

Premise 2: It requires multi-factor authentication.

Justification: The word Moreover tells us that both the security encryption and the authentication are premises. They are "stacking" evidence to support the final conclusion flagged by "Therefore."

The Strategist's Warning

Indicator words are powerful, but they aren't foolproof. The LSAC knows you're looking for them, and they will occasionally use indicators as trap words when in reality the conclusion comes later.

Always verify the word’s function: Is it providing a reason, or is it just a filler?

In the next lesson, we’ll tackle the Support System—the method you use when the LSAC takes away your GPS and leaves you with a naked argument.

Recap: Indicator words are explicit markers for an argument's anatomy. Conclusion Indicators (including pivots like but and however) signal the main claim. Premise Indicators signal the evidence, with "Power Additives" like moreover and furthermore linking multiple sentences together as supporting facts. Learning to spot these allows you to map out an argument's structure in seconds.

summary: In the last lesson, we established that arguments can have indicator words that help you decipher the support structure. But let’s be real: the LSAC doesn't always make it easy. Sometimes, they strip away the "GPS" indicator words like therefore or because, leaving you with two s
text_content:
In the last lesson, we established that arguments can have indicator words that help you decipher the support structure. But let’s be real: the LSAC doesn't always make it easy. Sometimes, they strip away the "GPS" indicator words like therefore or because, leaving you with two sentences that seem equally important, or they lay down traps to lead you the wrong way.

When the indicators are missing, you need to determine which claims are supporting which.

The "Why" Test: The Strategist's Secret

If you’re ever unsure which sentence is the conclusion, you can use the "Why" Test. Find the sentence you suspect is the main point and ask yourself: "Why should I believe this?"

If the other claims in the passage answer that question, you’ve found your conclusion. If they don't, you’re looking at a premise or "background noise."

Imagine you have two claims from a business proposal:

Claim A: Our startup will be profitable by Q4.

Claim B: Our customer acquisition cost has dropped by 40%.

Which one is the conclusion? We test the direction by plugging them into a "Therefore" format:

Option 1: CAC dropped by 40%; therefore, we will be profitable by Q4. (B → A)

Option 2: We will be profitable by Q4; therefore, our CAC dropped by 40%. (A → B)

Option 1 makes logical sense. The drop in cost is a fact that supports the prediction of profit.

Option 2 sounds like a crazy person talking—becoming profitable doesn't magically go back in time and lower your costs. In this case, Claim A is the Conclusion.

Using Support Flow as a Tool

If the "Why" Test is your tool for dissecting individual sentences, Support Flow is your method for analyzing the entire stimulus as a single unit. In the lab, we don't just look for parts; we look for the logical intent. Every argument has a specific mission—it is trying to establish a "bottom line," and your job is to identify which statement is the final destination for every other idea in the paragraph.

Finding the "End of the Line"

Support is a one-way street. You are looking for the statement that receives logical energy from other claims but gives none back.

The Premises are the foundational data points. They exist solely to justify another claim.

The Conclusion is the "End of the Line." It is the only statement in the stimulus that doesn't serve as a reason for anything else.

Identifying the Argument's Mission

To find the flow, categorize the stimulus by its primary goal. Most LSAT arguments are designed to establish one of three things:

A Recommendation: The argument is trying to establish that an action should be taken (e.g., "The company should hire more staff").

A Prediction: The argument is trying to establish that a specific event will occur in the future (e.g., "The economy will likely cool").

A Value Judgment: The argument is trying to establish a subjective interpretation of facts (e.g., "This move was reckless").

By identifying what the argument is trying to establish as a whole, you can filter out the supporting evidence and zero in on the core claim immediately. If you see a recommendation in a sea of facts, that recommendation is almost certainly the "End of the Line".

Three Examples

Let's see how this works as the "noise" in the stimulus increases.

1. The Simple Support

The new software update has three major security flaws. We must delay the product launch.

Support Test: We have flaws; therefore, we should delay. (Makes sense).

Bottom Line: The "delay" is a recommendation (the Weight). The "flaws" are the reason why (the Pillar).

2. Another Variation

Increasing the marketing budget is the only way to save the brand. Sales have hit an all-time low this month.

Support Test: Sales are at an all-time low; therefore, we must increase the budget. (Makes sense).

Bottom Line: Notice the conclusion came first here. The "all-time low" is the evidence that supports the "only way to save" claim.

3. Multiple Premises

High-performance athletes require specialized recovery protocols. Therefore, since David is a professional triathlete, he needs a custom physical therapy routine.

Support Test: David is a triathlete + athletes need protocols; therefore, David needs a routine.

Bottom Line: This is harder because there are multiple pillars and a premise comes after “therefore” conclusion indicator. Both the "general rule" and the "specific fact" about David are working together to support the final recommendation.

In the next lesson, you’ll work on identifying premise and conclusion indicators, before dissecting argument structure yourself.

Recap: When the "GPS" of indicator words is missing, we find the Bottom Line to identify the argument's core. By applying the "Why" Test or looking for Support Flow, you can distinguish between the supporting premises and the main conclusion that acts as the "End of the Line".

summary: Welcome to your first set of rep work. In the lab, Think of this like a reflex drill to help you memorize these words. When you see a "GPS" word, your brain should immediately categorize it as either an Evidence Flag (Premise) or a Conclusive Flag (Conclusion).
text_content:
Welcome to your first set of rep work. In the lab, Think of this like a reflex drill to help you memorize these words. When you see a "GPS" word, your brain should immediately categorize it as either an Evidence Flag (Premise) or a Conclusive Flag (Conclusion).

Below are 20 indicator words and phrases you’ll encounter constantly. Your job is to identify what they’re signaling before you check the answer.

ThereforeAnswer: Conclusion Indicator. A standard conclusive flag telling you the main point is likely coming next.

Since

Answer: Premise Indicator. Used to introduce supporting facts or background data.

Moreover

Answer: Special Premise Indicator. A "Power Additive" that acts as logical glue, signaling that both the current claim and the one before it are premises.

Thus

Answer: Conclusion Indicator. A classic marker for the argument's destination.

Given that

Answer: Premise Indicator. Signals that the following information is an accepted fact used to support a claim.

However

Answer: Pivot Conclusion Indicator. Often used to "turn" the argument toward the author’s main point, often after presenting a counter-point or context.

Because

Answer: Premise Indicator. A primary "Evidence" flag signaling the "Why" behind a claim.

Consequently

Answer: Conclusion Indicator. Tells you that the following statement is the logical result of the preceding evidence.

Furthermore

Answer: Special Premise Indicator. Another "Power Additive" that links the current sentence and the previous one as combined evidence.

Clearly

Answer: Conclusion Indicator. An emphatic flag used by an author to signal their primary value judgment or interpretation.

Due to

Answer: Premise Indicator. Directly introduces the cause or evidence for a claim.

But

Answer: Pivot Conclusion Indicator. Like "however," it often signals the transition from background context to the author's actual conclusion.

For example

Answer: Premise Indicator. Signals that a specific instance is being provided to support a broader claim.

As a result

Answer: Conclusion Indicator. Signals the final destination of the logical chain.

In addition

Answer: Special Premise Indicator. An additive flag that groups the current information with previous evidence to support the same goal.

Hence

Answer: Conclusion Indicator. A punchy, formal marker for the "End of the Line".

For

Answer: Premise Indicator. When used to mean "because," it signals that a supporting reason is coming next.

Shows

Answer: Conclusion Indicator. In the structure "X shows Y," Y is the destination or conclusion the author wants you to reach.

For this reason

Answer: Premise Indicator. Points back to a reason already established to justify the conclusion.

Accordingly

Answer: Conclusion Indicator. Signals that the following claim is consistent with and supported by the previously stated facts.

Likewise

Answer: Special Premise Indicator. An additive indicator that tells you both the claim following it and the claim before it are likewise premises.

Also

Answer: Special Premise Indicator. Can act as "logical glue," marking the current sentence and the preceding one as premises.

Recap: Here is a high-volume structural reflex drill. By internalizing these "GPS" markers, you've moved from passive reading to active structural analysis. Whether it’s a standard flag, a pivot, or a special premise indicator, these words can allow you to map out an argument's Bottom Line in seconds.

summary: Now that you’ve mastered the GPS indicators, it’s time to apply that knowledge to actual arguments. In this drill, your mission is simple: identify the Conclusion (the Goal) and the Premises (the Evidence). Remember, the conclusion is the claim that receives all the logical suppo
text_content:
Now that you’ve mastered the GPS indicators, it’s time to apply that knowledge to actual arguments. In this drill, your mission is simple: identify the Conclusion (the Goal) and the Premises (the Evidence). Remember, the conclusion is the claim that receives all the logical support but gives none back—it is the bottom line.

Once you’ve decided on the argument structure, click the reveal button to check the answer.

Example #1

Since caffeine blocks adenosine receptors in the brain, drinking a double espresso will temporarily increase your focus. Therefore, you should have a coffee before your next deep-work session.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: You should have a coffee before your next deep-work session.

Premises: (1) Caffeine blocks adenosine receptors in the brain, and (2) drinking a double espresso will temporarily increase your focus.

Justification: The word Therefore flags the final recommendation as the conclusion. The first two claims provide the biological and functional evidence.

Example #2

High-intensity interval training (HIIT) burns more calories in a shorter period than steady-state cardio. Given that your schedule only allows for thirty minutes in the gym, HIIT is the most efficient workout for your goals.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: HIIT is the most efficient workout for your goals.

Premises: (1) HIIT burns more calories in a shorter period than steady-state cardio, and (2) your schedule only allows for thirty minutes in the gym.

Justification: The phrase Given that introduces a factual constraint. The final claim is a value judgment based on those facts.

Example #3

Our organic reach on Instagram has dropped by 40% over the last quarter. Consequently, we must shift our marketing budget toward paid LinkedIn advertisements.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: We must shift our marketing budget toward paid LinkedIn advertisements.

Premises: Our organic reach on Instagram has dropped by 40% over the last quarter.

Justification: Consequently is a classic "Goal Flag" that points to the necessary action or recommendation.

Example #4

Electric scooters decrease the reliance on cars for short-distance travel. Because they reduce overall traffic congestion, city councils should subsidize e-scooter programs.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: City councils should subsidize e-scooter programs.

Premises: (1) E-scooters decrease reliance on cars, and (2) they reduce overall traffic congestion.

Justification: The author uses Because to mark the second premise. The final claim is a policy recommendation (The Ask).

Example #5

Reviewing your notes immediately after a lecture increases long-term retention. Also, it allows you to identify gaps in your understanding while the information is fresh. So post-lecture review is a superior study method.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: Post-lecture review is a superior study method.

Premises: (1) Reviewing notes increases retention, and (2) it allows you to identify gaps in understanding.

Justification: The "Power Additive" Also tells you both the first and second claims are premises. So flags the final value judgment as the conclusion.

Example #6

Excavated pottery fragments found at the coastal Site A match the unique clay composition of the inland Site B. However, the pigments used in these fragments were derived from minerals found only at Site C, which is hundreds of miles away. This shows that the inhabitants of Site A were engaged in a complex, long-distance trade network.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: The inhabitants of Site A were engaged in a complex, long-distance trade network.

Premises: (1) Pottery at Site A matches Site B clay, and (2) the pigments are unique to Site C.

Justification: The word However acts as a pivot from the first fact to a more significant one. The phrase This shows that signals the author's final interpretation.

Example #7

In emerging markets with high intellectual property risk, a joint venture generally provides better legal standing than a simple licensing agreement. Since our target market has a history of property seizure and weak court systems, we should opt for a joint venture to protect our assets.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: We should opt for a joint venture to protect our assets.

Premises: (1) Joint ventures provide better standing in high-risk markets, and (2) the target market has weak courts and property seizure risks.

Justification: This is a Recommendation mission. The word Since introduces the factual evidence regarding the specific market, supporting the final conclusion

Example #8

The true annual rate of earnings on a savings account is the interest rate minus the rate of inflation. Consequently, if the inflation rate drops significantly, the interest rate can be reduced by a corresponding amount without there being any change in the true rate of earnings.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: If the inflation rate drops, the interest rate can be reduced by an equal amount without changing the true rate of earnings.

Premises: The true annual rate of earnings is the interest rate minus the inflation rate.

Justification: The first sentence establishes a mathematical rule (a premise). Consequently flags the conclusion, which is a prediction or deduction based on that rule.

Example #9

No action can be considered truly altruistic if the actor receives any psychological benefit from it. Given that volunteering almost invariably boosts the volunteer’s self-esteem, it follows that volunteering is not an act of pure altruism.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: Volunteering is not an act of pure altruism.

Premises: (1) No action is altruistic if it provides a benefit, and (2) volunteering boosts self-esteem.

Justification: This is a complex chain. Given that introduces the second premise. It follows that is a heavy conclusion indicator marking the "End of the Line".

Example #10

The sediment layers in the Arctic basin show a distinct increase in volcanic ash during the late Eocene period. These ash deposits are chemically identical to volcanic sites in the South Pacific. Because there is no evidence of local volcanic activity, atmospheric currents during that era must have been significantly stronger than previously estimated.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: Atmospheric currents during that era must have been significantly stronger than previously estimated.

Premises: (1) Ash increased in the Arctic during the Eocene, (2) the ash matches South Pacific sites, and (3) there was no local volcanic activity.

Justification: The author uses Because to introduce the final premise. The conclusion is a Value Judgment/Interpretation that receives logical energy from other claims but gives none back.

summary: Welcome to the deep end. These arguments are longer, the language is denser, and the LSAC has laid down "Trap" Indicator Words to lead passive readers astray.
text_content:
Welcome to the deep end. These arguments are longer, the language is denser, and the LSAC has laid down "Trap" Indicator Words to lead passive readers astray.

Once you’ve decided on the argument structure, click the reveal button to check the answer.

Example #1

Most corporate restructuring plans fail because they focus exclusively on cost-cutting measures. Therefore, consultants often recommend that firms prioritize cultural alignment during a merger. However, culture cannot be meaningfully aligned unless the leadership team is willing to model new behaviors. Thus, the ultimate success of any merger depends entirely on the actions of the C-suite executives.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: The ultimate success of any merger depends entirely on the actions of the C-suite executives.

Premises: (1) Restructuring fails when focused only on costs, (2) consultants recommend prioritizing culture, and (3) culture requires leadership to model behaviors.

Justification: This is a classic trap. The word "Therefore" in the second sentence follows from the first sentence, but it is then used as a building block for the final claim. The Bottom Line is the last sentence, flagged by "Thus," which interprets the entire situation.

Example #2

Standardized testing was originally designed to provide a universal metric for student aptitude. Given that educational resources vary wildly between districts, these tests often reflect a student's socioeconomic background rather than their innate ability. Consequently, many universities are moving toward test-optional admissions. It follows that the influence of standardized testing on higher education is beginning to wane.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: The influence of standardized testing on higher education is beginning to wane.

Premises: (1) Testing was designed as a universal metric, (2) resources vary by district, (3) tests reflect background, and (4) universities are moving to test-optional models.

Justification: "Consequently" is a trap here. It marks a factual result (universities going test-optional), but that fact is then used to support the broader conclusion in the final sentence: that the influence is waning.

Example #3

Protecting the privacy of digital communications is essential for maintaining a free society. If the government is allowed to monitor encrypted messages without a warrant, whistleblowers will be less likely to expose corruption. Furthermore, the fear of surveillance invariably chills public discourse.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: Protecting the privacy of digital communications is essential for maintaining a free society.

Premises: (1) monitoring stops whistleblowers, and (2) surveillance chills discourse.

Justification: The author uses the "Power Additive" Furthermore to stack the premises. The first sentence is a Recommendation/Value Judgment as the goal of the argument.

Example #4

Heavy metals found in the runoff from the Silverton mine have contaminated the local watershed. Because these metals accumulate in the tissue of predatory fish, the local trout population has seen a massive decline in fertility. This shows that the mine's environmental impact is more severe than the company's initial reports suggested. For this reason, the provincial government must revoke the mine’s operating permit immediately.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: The provincial government must revoke the mine’s operating permit immediately.

Premises: (1) Heavy metals contaminated the watershed, (2) trout fertility declined, and (3) the impact is more severe than reported.

Justification: "This shows that" flags an intermediate interpretation. However, the final sentence is the Bottom Line because it is a Recommendation based on all the evidence that came before it.

Example #5

The city’s transit expansion project is destined for a budget overrun. After all, every major infrastructure project in this city’s history has exceeded its initial estimates by at least 20%. Moreover, the cost of raw materials has spiked since the project was approved. Clearly, the transit authority’s current financial projections are wildly optimistic.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: The city’s transit expansion project is destined for a budget overrun.

Premises: (1) Every major infrastructure project in this city’s history has exceeded its initial estimates by at least 20%, (2) the cost of raw materials has spiked since the project was approved, and (3) clearly, the transit authority’s current financial projections are wildly optimistic.

Justification: This argument follows a Conclusion First structure. The author leads with their main prediction. The phrase "After all" signals that the following sentences provide the evidence to justify that opening claim. While the word "Clearly" in the final sentence is typically a conclusion indicator, here it acts as a trap—it introduces a supporting value judgment that reinforces why the overrun is inevitable rather than being the final destination of the logic.

Example #6

Astronomers have detected high concentrations of methane in the atmosphere of the distant planet K-212. On Earth, such concentrations are almost exclusively produced by biological organisms. Thus, it is highly probable that some form of life exists on K-212. It follows that space agencies should prioritize sending a probe to this system over the upcoming lunar mission.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: Space agencies should prioritize sending a probe to this system over the upcoming lunar mission.

Premises: (1) Methane detected on K-212, (2) methane is usually produced by life, and (3) it's probable that life exists there.

Justification: "Thus" marks a sub-conclusion (that life probably exists). The entire argument then pivots to a Recommendation for the space agencies, which is the true Bottom Line.

Example #7

When a single firm controls the distribution network, they can artificially inflate prices to maximize profit. Given that the regional energy market is currently controlled by a single utility provider, consumers are likely being overcharged. Therefore, the regulatory board should intervene to enforce price caps.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: The regulatory board should intervene to enforce price caps.

Premises: (1) When a single firm controls the distributor network, they can inflate prices, (2) a single provider controls the current market, (3) consumers are likely being overcharged.

Justification: The final sentence is the Recommendation mission that receives all the logical support.

Example #8

Genetic sequencing of ancient canine remains from the Siberian permafrost revealed a high degree of similarity to modern gray wolves. Scientists further discovered that these specimens possessed a unique gene variant associated with cold adaptation. These findings show that the domestication of dogs likely occurred much earlier than previously thought, as this adaptation would have been unnecessary for dogs living alongside humans in warmer climates.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: The domestication of dogs likely occurred much earlier than previously thought.

Premises: (1) Remains match gray wolves, (2) they have a cold-adaptation gene, and (3) this adaptation is unnecessary for dogs living with humans in warm climates.

Justification: The phrase "These findings show that" identifies the Interpretation. The final clause (starting with "as") is actually a premise supporting the conclusion buried in the middle of the sentence .

Example #9

Since the ratio of home prices to median income is at a historic high, a market correction is inevitable. Furthermore, rising interest rates will soon decrease the pool of eligible buyers. Consequently, investors who are currently overleveraged in luxury properties face significant financial risk.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: Investors who are currently overleveraged in luxury properties face significant financial risk.

Premises: (1) Price-to-income ratio is at a historic high, (2) a correction is inevitable, and (3) rising rates will decrease buyers.

Justification: The final Value Judgment about the "financial risk" for investors is the Bottom Line.

Example #10

Technological progress in the field of renewable energy has significantly reduced the cost of solar panels. However, the efficiency of these panels remains limited by current battery storage capacity. If we cannot develop a more effective way to store energy for use during non-sunny hours, solar power will remain a secondary energy source. This indicates that the primary bottleneck for the green energy transition is storage technology, not panel production.

Reveal Button

Conclusion: The primary bottleneck for the green energy transition is storage technology, not panel production.

Premises: (1) Panel costs have reduced, (2) efficiency is limited by storage, and (3) solar will stay secondary without better storage.

Justification: The author uses "However" to pivot the focus. The "This indicates that" flag at the end identifies the final point of the entire stimulus.

Recap: You’ve successfully navigated the harder structures the LSAT has to offer. By looking past trap indicator words and focusing on the support flow, you've learned to identify the bottom line even when it's buried under technical detail.
source_heading: Module 2, Lesson 7: Rep Work: Identifying Premises and Conclusions (Hard)
module_label: Module 2: , Lesson 7: Rep Work: Identifying Premises and Conclusions (Hard)

summary: This Active Drill will be PT102.S2.Q1. This is a main conclusion question in regards to a psychiatrist's argument.
text_content:
This Active Drill will be PT102.S2.Q1. This is a main conclusion question in regards to a psychiatrist's argument.

The Question:

Psychiatrist: We are learning that neurochemical imbalances can cause behavior ranging from extreme mental illness to less serious but irritating behavior such as obsessive fantasizing, petulance, or embarrassment. These findings will promote compassion and tolerance when looking at a mental illness, quirk, or mere difference between two persons, since being mentally healthy can now begin to be seen as simply having the same neurochemical balances as most people.

Which one of the following most accurately expresses the conclusion of the psychiatrist's argument?

Understanding the role of the neurochemical in behavior will foster empathy toward others.

Neurochemical imbalances can cause mental illness and other behaviors.

Neurochemical balances and imbalances are the main determinants of mental behavior.

Being mentally healthy is a matter of having the same neurochemical balances as most people.

Advances in neurochemistry enhance our theories of mental illness.

We will input this question as the active drill. I will record a video explanation for this later but we will also have this written explanation for it.

First, is the analysis of the stimulus:

Stimulus Analysis

The psychiatrist begins by establishing a factual foundation: various behaviors, from severe illnesses to minor personality quirks, are rooted in neurochemical imbalances. However, the core of the argument is not the science itself, but a specific prediction about how this knowledge will influence human interaction.

The final sentence reveals the argument's destination by offering a reason for this shift in perspective. Since (premise indicator) being "mentally healthy" is now defined simply as having a common neurochemical balance, the psychiatrist concludes that these scientific findings will ultimately lead to increased compassion and tolerance. This prediction about emotional and social change is the bottom line for the logic.

Answer Choice Analysis (Place each explanation underneath each answer choice)

Choice

Verdict

Reason

A

CORRECT

This is a perfect paraphrase of the second claim. It identifies the Value Judgment that the findings will foster empathy (compassion).

B

Incorrect

This is a Premise. It’s the factual information provided in the very first sentence to set the stage. It supports the conclusion; it isn't the conclusion itself.

C

Incorrect

Too Strong and Not Found. The psychiatrist says neurochemistry can cause behavior, not that it is the main determinant. The LSAC loves to trap you with "extreme" language that goes beyond the stimulus.

D

Incorrect

This is a Premise. It’s the statement following our indicator word "since". It is the reason why we should be more compassionate.

E

Incorrect

Too Broad and Not Found. While the argument mentions neurochemistry, it’s specifically about the emotional result (compassion), not just a general improvement in medical theories.

summary: Up until now, we’ve been looking at arguments as simple, one-story buildings: you have a foundation (Premises) and a roof (The Conclusion) . But on the LSAT, the test-makers love to build skyscrapers.
text_content:
Up until now, we’ve been looking at arguments as simple, one-story buildings: you have a foundation (Premises) and a roof (The Conclusion) . But on the LSAT, the test-makers love to build skyscrapers.

In many stimuli, you will encounter a Sub-Conclusion (also called an intermediate conclusion). This is a statement that wears two hats: it is supported by premises, but it is then used to support the final Main conclusion.

The Argumentative Chain

Think of an argument with a sub-conclusion as a ladder. You can't reach the top step without stepping on the middle ones first.

Minor Premise: The raw data or core facts, supports the sub-conclusion.

Sub-Conclusion: A claim derived from the premise. It acts as support for main conclusion AND its backed by support from minor premise

Main Conclusion: The final "Goal" of the entire stimulus. It is backed by all the support.

In a chain of support, it looks like this:

Minor Premise → Sub-Conclusion → Main Conclusion

How to Tell Them Apart

The biggest trap in figuring our argument structure is picking an answer choice that is "true" according to the stimulus but is actually just a sub-conclusion. To avoid this, use the Support Flow tool.

The Main Conclusion is the only claim that doesn't support anything else. It is the final destination for all the logical energy in the paragraph.

The Sub-Conclusion is a "middle-man." It receives support from a premise, but then it turns around and pushes that energy toward the main conclusion.

Let's look at how adding one sentence changes the entire architecture of an argument, using this sample argument about the sun below:

Simple Structure: "We cannot go to the sun because the sun is hot."

Main Conclusion: We cannot go to the sun.

Regular Premise: The sun is hot.

Complex Structure: "We cannot go to the sun because the sun is hot. This is because it is 10,000 degrees."

Main Conclusion: We cannot go to the sun.

Sub-Conclusion: The sun is hot.

Minor Premise: It is 10,000 degrees.

In the complex version, "The sun is hot" is no longer just a fact; it is a claim that now requires its own evidence (the temperature) to be proven before it can help prove we can't visit.

When an argument includes a sub-conclusion rather than just independent premises and a conclusion, we call this a Complex Argument

The "Sub-Conclusion Trap"

The LSAC uses sub-conclusions to create "Intermediate Traps." They will place a conclusion indicator like "thus" or "therefore" in front of a sub-conclusion to trick you into thinking you've found the main point.

As a strategist, you must always ask: "Is the author trying to prove this point, or are they using this point to prove something else?" If it’s being used as evidence for a later claim, keep moving until you hit the Bottom Line.

The Solution: If you are stuck between two claims, use the Flow Test.

Ask: "Does Claim A support Claim B, or does Claim B support Claim A?".

The statement that receives the support but doesn't give any away is your Main Conclusion.

A Quick Warning: Be careful not to pick "Valid Inferences" (things that could be true) or bring your own assumptions into the text when analyzing support structure. The Main Conclusion must be the exact point the author is trying to establish as a whole, stick only to the text.

Recap: A sub-conclusion is a claim that is supported by a “minor” premise but also acts as a premise for the main conclusion. To identify the Main Conclusion, look for the statement that receives support but provides none to any other part of the argument—the true Bottom Line .
source_heading: Module 2, Lesson 10: Building the Ladder: Sub-Conclusions
module_label: Module 2: , Lesson 10: Building the Ladder: Sub-Conclusions
