summary: It is time to put your architectural skills to the test. In this drill, we are moving beyond simple arguments and into multi-story structures. Your task is to identify the different pieces of complex argument: the Minor Premise, the Sub-Conclusion, the Main Conclusion.
text_content:
It is time to put your architectural skills to the test. In this drill, we are moving beyond simple arguments and into multi-story structures. Your task is to identify the different pieces of complex argument: the Minor Premise, the Sub-Conclusion, the Main Conclusion.

Do this for each example below, and then click the reveal button to see the correct answer.

Example #1

"Mastering the fundamental defensive positions is the hallmark of a Jiu Jitsu blue belt. Because David has earned his blue belt, he has clearly mastered those fundamentals. It follows that he is capable of defending himself in most physical confrontations."

Reveal Button

Main Conclusion: David is capable of defending himself in most physical confrontations.

Sub-Conclusion: David has clearly mastered those fundamentals.

Minor Premises: David has earned his blue belt & Mastering fundamental defensive positions is the hallmark of a Jiu Jitsu blue belt.

Justification: The fact that he is a blue belt (Minor Premise) proves he mastered fundamentals (Sub-Conclusion). That sub-conclusion supports the final prediction about his defensive capability.

Example #2

The cost of legal research software has tripled since the firm signed its last contract. This drastic price hike means that the firm’s overhead costs will exceed the annual budget. Therefore, the partners must implement a temporary hiring freeze.

Reveal Button

Main Conclusion: The partners must implement a temporary hiring freeze.

Sub-Conclusion: The firm’s overhead costs will exceed the annual budget.

Minor Premise: The cost of legal research software has tripled since the firm signed its last contract.

Justification: The specific software cost (Minor Premise) leads to a broader conclusion about the overall budget (Sub-Conclusion). The budget deficit then justifies the final recommendation to freeze hiring (Main Conclusion).

Example #3

The partners must implement a temporary hiring freeze. This is because the firm’s overhead costs are set to exceed the annual budget, given that the cost of legal research software has tripled since the last contract was signed.

Reveal Button

Main Conclusion: The partners must implement a temporary hiring freeze.

Sub-Conclusion: The firm’s overhead costs are set to exceed the annual budget.

Minor Premise: The cost of legal research software has tripled.

Justification: The argument leads with the Main Conclusion (The "Ask"). It then provides a reason (Sub-Conclusion), which itself is backed up by a factual price hike (Minor Premise).

Example #4

The fragments found in the pit must be of relatively recent origin. This is clear because the eggshell fragments show almost no sign of amino acid decomposition. It follows that the archaeological site was inhabited much later than previous estimates suggested.

Reveal Button

Main Conclusion: The site was inhabited much later than previous estimates suggested.

Sub-Conclusion: The fragments must be of relatively recent origin.

Minor Premise: The fragments show almost no sign of amino acid decomposition.

Justification: The lack of decomposition (Minor Premise) is used to prove the age of the fragments (Sub-Conclusion). That age is then used to support the final claim about the site's history (Main Conclusion).

Example #5

The government must pass new cybersecurity legislation before the end of the decade. This is necessary because our current data protection laws are becoming obsolete. We know this because modern encryption algorithms are now vulnerable to being cracked by quantum computers.

Reveal Button

Main Conclusion: The government must pass new cybersecurity legislation.

Sub-Conclusion: Our current data protection laws are becoming obsolete.

Minor Premise: Modern encryption algorithms are vulnerable to being cracked.

Justification: The technical vulnerability (Minor Premise) is the "Why" for the obsolescence (Sub-Conclusion). The obsolescence is the "Why" for the final legislative recommendation (Main Conclusion).

Example #6

Venture capital funding for consumer electronics has dropped to its lowest level in a decade, which will force many hardware startups to shut down their operations. Consequently, the pace of innovation in the smartphone market is destined to slow down.

Reveal Button

Main Conclusion: The pace of innovation in the smartphone market is destined to slow down.

Sub-Conclusion: Many hardware startups will be forced to shut down.

Minor Premise: VC funding for consumer electronics has dropped to a decade low.

Justification: A macro-economic fact (Minor Premise) is used to predict startup failure (Sub-Conclusion). That failure is the only reason provided for the final prediction about market innovation (Main Conclusion).

Example #7

The current rate of water consumption is unsustainable. We can see that consumption is too high because the city's reservoir has fallen below 20% capacity. Accordingly, the city council must mandate strict water rationing for all residents immediately.

Reveal Button

Main Conclusion: The city council must mandate strict water rationing.

Sub-Conclusion: The current rate of water consumption is unsustainable.

Minor Premise: The city's reservoir has fallen below 20% capacity.

Justification: The physical water level (Minor Premise) proves the "unsustainability" (Sub-Conclusion). The unsustainability then supports the final recommendation (Main Conclusion).

Example #8

Now is an unsuitable time for first-time buyers to enter the real estate market. A significant market correction is imminent. The ratio of home prices to median income has reached a historic high.

Reveal Button

Main Conclusion: Now is an unsuitable time for first-time buyers to enter the market.

Sub-Conclusion: A significant market correction is imminent.

Minor Premise: The ratio of home prices to median income has reached a historic high.

Justification: This "naked" argument relies on the "Why" Test. Why is it an unsuitable time? Because a crash is coming (Sub-Conclusion). Why is a crash coming? Because of the historic ratio (Minor Premise).

Example #9

Most individuals still use the same password for their email and banking accounts. This behavior makes a massive surge in identity theft likely. Therefore, banks must implement mandatory biometric authentication for all online transactions.

Reveal Button

Main Conclusion: Banks must implement mandatory biometric authentication.

Sub-Conclusion: A massive surge in identity theft is likely.

Minor Premise: Most individuals use the same password for email and banking.

Justification: The specific user habit (Minor Premise) proves the threat of theft (Sub-Conclusion). That threat then justifies the final recommendation for biometric security (Main Conclusion).

Example #10

The legal landscape for tech companies is undergoing a permanent shift. This is shown by the fact that the Supreme Court’s ruling on digital privacy will force lower courts to overturn several recent convictions. This is because the ruling significantly expands the definition of "unreasonable search and seizure."

Reveal Button

Main Conclusion: The legal landscape for tech companies is undergoing a permanent shift.

Sub-Conclusion: Lower courts will be forced to overturn several recent convictions.

Minor Premise: The ruling significantly expands the definition of "unreasonable search and seizure."

Justification: The expansion of the definition (Minor Premise) provides the legal "Why" for the overturned convictions (Sub-Conclusion). Those specific legal changes are then used to justify the broader interpretation that the entire landscape is shifting (Main Conclusion).

summary: On the LSAT, not every sentence is meaningful. In many stimuli, the test-makers include sentences that do absolutely no logical lifting. They don't support the conclusion, and they aren't the conclusion itself. In the lab, we call this The Noise or “Context”.
text_content:
On the LSAT, not every sentence is meaningful. In many stimuli, the test-makers include sentences that do absolutely no logical lifting. They don't support the conclusion, and they aren't the conclusion itself. In the lab, we call this The Noise or “Context”.

Context is information that is related to the topic (which can be important) but sits outside the Logical Heart of the argument. While it is necessary for you to understand what is being discussed, it is irrelevant to how the argument is proven.

The Two Common Flavors of Context

Context generally falls into two categories. Learning to spot these allows you to mentally "delete" them so you can focus on the actual mechanics of the question.

1. Setting the Stage

This is background information used to introduce a topic or provide historical data. It "sets the table" so the argument has a place to sit.

Example: "For decades, Harvard Business School has utilized a specific grading curve for its core finance courses. Recently, the administration decided to review this policy. Because student stress levels have reached an all-time high, the curve should be abolished."

The Landscape: The first two sentences are just telling you the history of the curve and the fact that a review is happening.

The Argument: The conclusion is that the curve should be abolished, supported by the premise that student stress is too high.

2. The Rival Pitch (Other People's Positions)

The LSAT loves to report on what "some people" or "critics" believe. Often, the author only mentions these views so they can knock them down.

Example: "Many venture capitalists believe that a startup's success is determined solely by the pedigree of its founders. But this is clearly false. Our data shows that market timing is actually the primary determinant of long-term profitability."

The Rival Pitch: "Many venture capitalists believe..." This isn't the author's view; it’s the setup .

The Turn: The word "But" signals that the author is about to start their actual argument.

The GPS for Noise: Context Indicators

Just like premises and conclusions, context has its own signposts. When you see these, it is more than likely you are about to read the noise.

Type of Context

Common Phrases

Other People’s Positions

"Some people claim...", "Critics argue...", "It has been suggested that...", "Tradition holds that..."

Historical Data/Facts

"Historically...", "In the past...", "For many years...", "Research has shown that..."

The Pivot

But, However, Yet. These words often mark the boundary where the context ends and the author's argument begins .

The "Delete" Test

If you are unsure if a sentence is context or a premise, try the Delete Test.

Ask yourself: "If I remove this sentence, does the conclusion lose its factual support?"

If the conclusion still has its "Why," the sentence was just Context.

If the conclusion is now floating without any evidence, that sentence was a Premise.

Strategist's Rule: Top scorers spend less time reading the context and more time dissecting the relationship between the premises and the conclusion. Don't fall in love with the "story"—find the "structure."

Recap: Context is "The Noise"—information that provides a background or introduces a rival position but does not play a role in the logical reasoning. You can use various context indicators to decipher when you are reading noise.
source_heading: Module 2, Lesson 12: The Noise: Context & Background
module_label: Module 2: , Lesson 12: The Noise: Context & Background

lesson_title: Rep Work: Filtering for the Core
summary: In this exercise, we take stimuli and reduce them to their argumentative core: Premises and Main Conclusion.
text_content:
In this exercise, we take stimuli and reduce them to their argumentative core: Premises and Main Conclusion.

Do this for each example below, and then click the reveal button to see the correct answer.

Example #1

Recent changes in personal injury legislation were designed to lower premiums for Ontario drivers. The government initially stated that these reforms would simplify the claims process for those involved in minor accidents. However, the legislation has actually created a barrier to access for the average citizen, because the new rules impose a strict 24-hour reporting window that most claimants cannot meet.

Reveal Button

The Noise (Context): The legislative intent and the government's initial public statements.

The Heart:

Premise: The new rules impose a strict 24-hour reporting window that most claimants cannot meet.

Main Conclusion: The legislation has created a barrier to access for the average citizen.

Justification: The first two sentences are "Table Setting" context. The word "However" pivots to the author’s conclusion, which is then backed by the factual reporting window premise.

Example #2

Many academic advisors at business schools suggest that students should focus their electives on traditional finance and accounting modules. These advisors believe that a narrow specialization is the most direct path to a high-paying corporate role. But this strategy is fundamentally flawed. Our placement data shows that graduates with a diverse background in ethics and global strategy are recruited more frequently by top-tier consulting firms.

Reveal Button

The Noise (Context): The "Rival Pitch" from academic advisors regarding narrow specialization.

The Heart:

Premise: Graduates with a diverse background are recruited more frequently by top-tier consulting firms.

Main Conclusion: The strategy of focusing electives solely on traditional finance and accounting is fundamentally flawed.

Justification: The author introduces an opposing view only to knock it down. The word "But" signals the turn toward the author's Value Judgment mission.

Example #3

Saltwater aquarists often spend thousands of dollars on specialized lighting systems designed to mimic the solar spectrum. While these lights are aesthetically pleasing, a high-quality protein skimmer is the most vital component of a healthy reef tank. This is because the removal of organic waste is the only way to prevent the toxic ammonia spikes that kill sensitive marine life.

Reveal Button

The Noise (Context): The cost and aesthetic appeal of lighting systems.

The Heart:

Premise: The removal of organic waste is the only way to prevent toxic ammonia spikes.

Main Conclusion: A high-quality protein skimmer is the most vital component of a healthy reef tank.

Justification: The first sentence is background info. The "While" clause is a concession. The heart is a Recommendation mission justified by biological necessity.

Example #4

Historically, the legal profession has relied on manual document review to identify relevant case law. This process required junior associates to spend hundreds of hours in physical archives. Yet, this traditional method is no longer viable in the age of digital litigation. Modern AI-powered search tools can process thousands of pages in seconds, identifying key precedents with a higher degree of accuracy than a human researcher.

Reveal Button

The Noise (Context): The history and labor-intensive nature of manual document review.

The Heart:

Premise: Modern AI-powered search tools process thousands of pages in seconds with higher accuracy than human researchers.

Main Conclusion: The traditional method of manual document review is no longer viable.

Justification: The word "Yet" pivots from the historical context to the author’s take of current reality.

Example #5

The concentration of methane in the atmosphere of planet K-212 is triple the levels found on Earth. Methane is almost exclusively produced as a byproduct of biological activity. Space agencies will prioritize a mission to K-212 over the scheduled lunar landing.

Reveal Button

The Noise (Context): The specific concentration levels of methane.

The Heart:

Premise: Methane is almost exclusively produced as a byproduct of biological activity.

Main Conclusion: Space agencies will prioritize a mission to K-212 over the scheduled lunar landing.

Justification: The first sentence sets the stage. The relationship is between a biological rule (Premise) and a Prediction of future agency behavior (Conclusion).

module_label: Module 2: , Lesson 13: Rep Work: Filtering for the Core

lesson_title: Active Drill: St. Patrick’s Day Carnations
summary: This Active Drill will be PT135.S1.Q2. This is a main conclusion question in regards to a florist’s argument.
text_content:
This Active Drill will be PT135.S1.Q2. This is a main conclusion question in regards to a florist’s argument.

Florist: Some people like to have green carnations on St. Patrick's Day. But flowers that are naturally green are extremely rare. Thus, it is very difficult for plant breeders to produce green carnations. Before St. Patrick's Day, then, it is wise for florists to stock up on white carnations, which are fairly inexpensive and quite easy to dye green.

Which one of the following most accurately expresses the overall conclusion of the florist's argument?

It is a good idea for florists to stock up on white carnations before St. Patrick's Day.

Flowers that are naturally green are very rare.

There are some people who like to have green carnations on St. Patrick's Day.

White carnations are fairly inexpensive and can easily be dyed green.

It is very difficult to breed green carnations.

We will input this question as the active drill. I will record a video explanation for this later but we will also have this written explanation for it.

First, is the analysis of the stimulus:

Stimulus Analysis

The florist begins with a piece of Context: "Some people like to have green carnations on St. Patrick's Day." This is purely a background fact that identifies the market demand; the author isn't trying to prove people like green flowers—they are just reporting it. From there, the argument splits into two distinct lines of reasoning to reach a single Recommendation which is “Before St. Patrick's Day, then, it is wise for florists to stock up on white carnations.”

The first line of reasoning rules out green carnations by building a ladder of support: a Minor Premise (green flowers are rare) justifies a Sub-conclusion (breeding them is difficult).

The second line of reasoning provides two Independent Premises for an alternative solution: white carnations are both inexpensive and easy to dye. All three pieces of evidence—the difficulty of breeding green flowers and the two benefits of white ones—point directly to the Main Conclusion: it is wise to stock up on white carnations.

Answer Choice Analysis (Place each explanation underneath each answer choice)

Choice

Verdict

The Strategist's Reason

A

CORRECT

This is the Main Conclusion. It is the "Ask" or the Recommendation mission that all other sentences work to support .

B

Incorrect

This is a Minor Premise. It is a factual statement used only to prove the sub-conclusion about the difficulty of breeding.

C

Incorrect

This is Context. It sets the table by identifying the topic, but it is not a claim being proven by the author.

D

Incorrect

These are Regular Premises. They are independent reasons why white carnations are a good alternative, supporting the main conclusion directly.

E

Incorrect

This is a Sub-conclusion. While it is signaled by the indicator word "Thus" making it the trap choice of the question, it is actually a "middle-man" sub-conclusion used to justify the final recommendation of stocking up on white carnations.
source_heading: Module 2, Lesson 14: Active Drill: St. Patrick’s Day Carnations
module_label: Module 2: , Lesson 14: Active Drill: St. Patrick’s Day Carnations

course_description: Core BetterLSAT syllabus imported from DOCX
summary: In previous lessons, we learned to filter out The Noise—information that sets the table or describes what others believe. But there is a more subtle type of noise that the LSAC uses to trip up test-takers: The Concession.
text_content:
In previous lessons, we learned to filter out The Noise—information that sets the table or describes what others believe. But there is a more subtle type of noise that the LSAC uses to trip up test-takers: The Concession.

A concession is a specific type of context where the author admits to a fact that actually cuts against their own argument. While a premise is there to support the conclusion, a concession is there to acknowledge a counter-point before moving on to the main goal.

The Strategy: Why Admit Weakness?

You might wonder why an author would include information that makes their own case look weaker. In the world of high-level logic, this is called "Stealing the Thunder."

By volunteering a potential objection, the author:

Anticipates the Opponent: They address the obvious counter-argument before you (the reader) can even think of it.

Increases Credibility: It makes the author appear more objective and balanced.

Clears the Path: Once the author admits the negative fact, they can say, "Yes, that is true, BUT my conclusion still holds for these other reasons."

The GPS: Concession Indicators

Just like premises and conclusions, concessions have their own "flag" words. When you see these, your brain should immediately label the clause as "Noise" that goes against the author's Bottom Line.

Although / Though / Even though

While (when used to mean "although")

Despite / In spite of

Notwithstanding

Even if

Granted / Granted that

Admittedly

It is true that

Concessions in the Lab

Let’s look at how a concession sits inside a support relationship. Notice how the concession doesn't change the End of the Line; it just acknowledges a hurdle.

Example #1: The Business Strategy

"Admittedly, increasing the minimum wage will raise our quarterly overhead. However, it makes sense to do so because higher wages lead to increased employee retention, which lowers long-term training costs."

Concession: Increasing the minimum wage will raise overhead. (This hurts the argument for raising wages).

Premise: Higher wages lead to increased retention and lower training costs. (The "Why").

Main Conclusion: It makes sense to increase the minimum wage. (The "Goal").

Example #2: The Law School Application

"Carl should still apply to Yale even though they have an incredibly low acceptance rate, given that his 175 LSAT score and legal experience as a paralegal make him a top-tier candidate."

Concession: Yale has an incredibly low acceptance rate. (A reason not to apply).

Premises: (1) Carl has a 175 LSAT, and (2) he has legal experience as a paralegal.

Main Conclusion: Carl should still apply.

The "Label and Move" Rule

When you encounter a concession indicator, do not try to fit that information into your support chain. Treat it as noise.

Identify the Indicator: Spot words like although or despite.

Label it Context: Acknowledge it is a counter-point.

Find the Pivot: Look for the word that brings you back to the author’s side (usually but or however) .

Locate the argument: Focus only on the premises that actually support the conclusion.

Take a stab at it yourself in the next Rep Work lesson.

Recap: A concession is a type of contextual "Noise" where the author acknowledges a point that weakens their case. By using Concession Indicators like although or notwithstanding, authors steal the thunder of potential objections. Your job is to label these points as counter-data and focus on the Support Flow that leads to the author's true bottom line.

summary: Time to identify the Logical Heart while acknowledging the Concession—the piece of information that the author admits to, even though it cuts against their final goal.
text_content:
Time to identify the Logical Heart while acknowledging the Concession—the piece of information that the author admits to, even though it cuts against their final goal.

Do this for each example below, and then click the reveal button to see the correct answer.

Example #1

Although the cost of living in downtown Toronto is among the highest in North America, accepting an offer from Osgoode Hall is the most strategic move for a future human rights lawyer. The school’s proximity to international NGOs and high-profile advocacy groups provides networking opportunities that simply do not exist elsewhere.

Reveal Button

Concession: The cost of living in downtown Toronto is among the highest in North America.

Premise: The school’s proximity to international NGOs and high-profile advocacy groups provides unique networking opportunities.

Main Conclusion: Accepting an offer from Osgoode Hall is the most strategic move.

Justification: The author uses "Although" to acknowledge a significant downside (cost) before pivoting to the professional benefits (Premise) that justify the final recommendation (Conclusion).

Example #2

Admittedly, a premium cloud-storage subscription costs more per month than basic storage plans. However, professionals should opt for the premium tier because the integrated encryption features significantly reduce the risk of costly data breaches.

Reveal Button

Concession: A premium cloud-storage subscription costs more per month than basic storage plans.

Premise: The integrated encryption features significantly reduce the risk of costly data breaches.

Main Conclusion: Professionals should opt for the premium tier.

Justification: The author "steals the thunder" of the price objection by admitting it first. The word "However" signals the turn to the author's actual argument .

Example #3

We know the new medication causes mild drowsiness in a small percentage of patients during the first week of treatment. But, it is a breakthrough in the management of chronic insomnia. Clinical trials have demonstrated that the drug restores natural sleep cycles without the risk of long-term dependency.

Reveal Button

Concession: The new medication causes mild drowsiness in some patients.

Premise: Clinical trials have demonstrated that the drug restores natural sleep cycles without the risk of long-term dependency.

Main Conclusion: It is a breakthrough in the management of chronic insomnia.

Justification: Without "GPS" words, we look for the Support Flow. The author admits a side effect (Concession) but establishes that the restoration of sleep cycles (Premise) proves the drug is a "breakthrough" (Conclusion).

Example #4

Launching a product in a saturated market requires a massive initial marketing spend. Yet this is a strategic necessity for established brands looking to maintain their market share. Failing to defend a dominant position allows smaller competitors to erode the brand's long-term customer base.

Reveal Button

Concession: Launching a product in a saturated market requires a massive initial marketing spend.

Premise: Failing to defend a dominant position allows smaller competitors to erode the brand's long-term customer base.

Main Conclusion: This is a strategic necessity for established brands looking to maintain their market share.

Justification: The author admits to a high cost (Concession) but argues that the risk of losing market share (Premise) makes the marketing spend a "strategic necessity" (Conclusion).

Example #5

Local governments must prioritize implementing a city-wide composting program, even though these programs involve high logistical costs and require extensive public education. Diverting organic waste from landfills is the only viable way to meet the state's aggressive carbon-reduction targets.

Reveal Button

Concession: The programs involve high logistical costs and require extensive public education.

Premise: Diverting organic waste from landfills is the only viable way to meet the state's carbon-reduction targets.

Main Conclusion: Local governments must prioritize implementing a city-wide composting program.

Justification: The author admits the costs and social friction but concludes the programs are a "must" because they are the only way to solve the primary problem of carbon reduction.

lesson_title: The Missing Link: Assumptions and the Continuum of Support
summary: Up to this point, we have treated arguments like visible structures: you see the Premise and you see the Conclusion. But in the lab, it is important for the LSAT prodigy to know that the most important part of an argument is often what you can't see.
text_content:
Up to this point, we have treated arguments like visible structures: you see the Premise and you see the Conclusion. But in the lab, it is important for the LSAT prodigy to know that the most important part of an argument is often what you can't see.

An Assumption is the "Missing Link" between the evidence and the bottom line. It is an unstated claim that the author takes for granted. If the assumption is true, the bridge holds; if the assumption is false, the entire argument collapses.

The Concept of the "Gap"

Every LSAT argument is essentially a leap from a set of facts to a specific claim. The space between where the evidence ends and the conclusion begins is the Gap.

Consider this basic example:

"The air conditioning in this apartment is currently broken. Therefore, the apartment is completely unlivable."

The Premise: The air conditioning is broken.

The Conclusion: The apartment is completely unlivable.

The Gap: The author is jumping from a broken appliance to a quality of life judgment.

The Assumption: A functioning air conditioner is a necessary requirement for an apartment to be considered livable. Notice that the author never stated this link. They simply acted as if a lack of AC guarantees unlivability. If it turns out the apartment is in a cold climate where AC is never needed, the "leap" fails.

Support Strength and Reasonableness

On the LSAT, it is your job to understand how assumptions can affect arguments. This is evaluated on how reasonable assumptions made in the argument are and how many of them there are. The strength of an argument is inversely related to the size and quality of its assumptions.

The Calculus of Strength:

Strong Arguments: Have fewer and more reasonable assumptions. These are gaps that are easy to cross because the unstated information is almost certainly true.

Weak Arguments: Have more or less reasonable assumptions. These are large "craters" in logic where the author is taking a massive, debatable leap.

Evaluating "Reasonableness"

Reasonable Assumptions: These would be widely accepted facts or logical necessities.

Example: If the apartment is in the middle of a desert during summer, assuming AC is needed for livability is reasonable. Most people would agree.

Unreasonable Assumptions: These would be subjective, extreme, or may ignore other possibilities.

Example: If the apartment is in a mild climate, assuming the lack of AC makes it "completely unlivable" is unreasonable. It ignores other factors like open windows or fans.

So, the more work an audience has to do to "fill in the blanks" for an author, the weaker the argument becomes. A perfectly strong argument (a deduction) leaves no blanks at all.

Side Note: The "Negation" Test

When we eventually get to necessary assumption questions, the ultimate way to identify an assumption that the argument MUST rely on is to use the Negation Test (you will use this later). If you stipulate that an assumption is false, and the conclusion no longer follows from the premises, you have found the "Missing Link."

Argument: "This car has a 5-star safety rating. Therefore, it is the best choice for a teenager's first vehicle."

The Assumption: Safety is a primary factor in determining the "best choice" for a new driver.

The Wrecking Test: What if safety is not a primary factor? What if the "best choice" is determined by affordability or fuel economy?

The Result: The argument is wrecked. The 5-star safety rating is still a "fact," but it no longer proves that this car is the "best" choice. The bridge has collapsed because the unstated link (the assumption) was removed.

Let’s take a deeper dive into an argument and its assumptions in the next lesson.

Recap: While some arguments have no gaps, most LSAT arguments rely on Assumptions—unstated claims taken for granted to connect the premises to the conclusion. Your job as a strategist is to measure that gap. A strong argument relies on few reasonable Assumptions, while a weak argument relies on many unreasonable ones.
source_heading: Module 2, Lesson 17: The Missing Link: Assumptions and the Continuum of Support
module_label: Module 2: , Lesson 17: The Missing Link: Assumptions and the Continuum of Support

lesson_title: The Case Study: The Remote Work Policy
summary: In the previous lesson, we established the theory: Assumptions are the unstated logical tissue that connects your premises to your conclusion. Now, we’re going to perform a deep-dive dissection of a single argument to see exactly how these "Missing Links" can either hold up a str
text_content:
In the previous lesson, we established the theory: Assumptions are the unstated logical tissue that connects your premises to your conclusion. Now, we’re going to perform a deep-dive dissection of a single argument to see exactly how these "Missing Links" can either hold up a structure or cause it to collapse.

The Case Study: The Remote Work Policy

Consider this argument:

Our company should allow all employees to work from home indefinitely. This is because a recent internal survey of staff found that 80% reported feeling more productive when working remotely.

The Premise (The Fact): 80% of staff reported feeling more productive while working remotely.

The Conclusion (The Recommendation): The company should allow indefinite remote work.

Does this follow 100%? What assumptions is this argument making? Here’s a couple…

The author is jumping from a feeling to a permanent corporate policy. This is a massive leap. Here are some key unstated assumptions this argument makes:

Assumption A: The employees' self-reported feelings of productivity accurately reflect their actual work output.

Assumption B: Productivity is a significant and important factor that the company should consider when deciding on work-from-home policies.

Assumption C: The staff members who responded to the internal survey are representative of the company’s staff as a whole.

Now, as we said. Not all assumptions are the same. Some are reasonable and some aren’t. Let’s evaluate each:

Assumption A: The employees' self-reported feelings of productivity accurately reflect their actual work output.

This assumption is pretty unreasonable. In the world of evidence, self-reported data is a notoriously unreliable source of information. Because employees are judging themselves, their perception is often biased. People often feel more productive when they are comfortable, but their actual output might be lower. The author takes for granted that the "feeling" equals the "fact."

They might feel "productive" because they enjoy the comfort of their home environment, while their actual measurable output remains the same or even decreases. Since the author relies on a subjective feeling to prove an objective result, this is a major leap to rely on, making it a point of weakness for this argument.

Assumption B: Productivity is a significant and important factor that the company should consider when deciding on work-from-home policies.

Now this assumption is reasonable. Productivity is a fundamental metric for any business, so it is logically sound to treat it as an important factor in policy decisions. Crucially, the argument does not claim that productivity is the only factor the company should care about; it simply assumes it is a relevant and important one.

Because productivity is an established priority in business contexts, this is a safe, small gap to cross that would be widely accepted.

Assumption C: The staff members who responded to the internal survey are representative of the company’s staff as a whole.

Before we evaluate this last link, I want to tell you something important. When we look at arguments, we must apply a Principle of Charity. This is a principle that requires us to interpret an argument in its best possible light (or charitably) rather than assuming things ourselves to destroy it. Unless we have a specific reason to believe a sample is biased we generally assume that a survey meant to represent a group actually does so.

So if we found out that only those who are highly accountable and self-disciplined took the time to fill out the survey— and they only account for 5% of staff—then this would give us reason to believe the survey wasn’t representative of everyone, thereby undermining the conclusion.

But, we don’t have that reason. In this case, we apply the Principle of Charity and say this is a reasonable assumption to make.

The Power of Filling In the Blanks

Now to stress how important assumptions are on the LSAT. I want to do an experiment with you. Confirming assumptions the argument makes as facts will help strengthen your argument, and finding out an assumption is false can completely destroy it.

Let’s take an assumption and "promote" it to an explicit premise. By stating the missing link as a fact, we eliminate the logical leap.

Our company should allow all employees to work from home indefinitely. This is because a recent internal survey of staff found that 80% reported feeling more productive when working remotely. Internal data confirms that employee self-reports are an accurate reflection of their actual productivity levels.

By plugging that gap, we’ve removed the biggest vulnerability. The critic can no longer say, "But feelings aren't facts!" because we've explicitly tied them together through facts.

Conversely, the easiest way to Weaken an argument is to show that a load-bearing assumption is actually false. If we stipulate that the missing link doesn't exist, the bridge collapses.

Our company should allow all employees to work from home indefinitely. A recent survey found 80% of staff feel more productive remotely. Studies show that employees are consistently inaccurate when judging their own productivity levels.

See how the argument just got 10x worse? The premise (the feeling) is still a "fact," but it is now logically useless. It provides zero support for the conclusion because we’ve proven that the feeling doesn't actually lead to the result.

Recap: This case study has provided a full-circle view of how Assumptions function as the "Missing Links" in an argument. We’ve learned that:

The Gap: Most arguments contain a jump where the premise ends and the conclusion begins.

Identification: We can tease out specific assumptions (Accuracy, Relevance, Representativeness) that the author is taking for granted.

Reasonableness: Not all assumptions are equal. We must separate unreasonable leaps from reasonable ones, while using the Principle of Charity to remain fair to the author's sample.

Manipulation: You can Strengthen an argument by making its assumptions explicit, or Weaken it by finding an assumption is false.

summary: So far we’ve learned that "strong" isn't a personality trait of an argument—it is a technical measure of the bond between the premises and the conclusion. To master the LSAT, you must move away from thinking of support as a "yes or no" toggle. Instead, think of it as a Continuum.
text_content:
So far we’ve learned that "strong" isn't a personality trait of an argument—it is a technical measure of the bond between the premises and the conclusion. To master the LSAT, you must move away from thinking of support as a "yes or no" toggle. Instead, think of it as a Continuum.

To determine where an argument sits on this line, we play a mental game: imagine a world where the premises are 100% true. Now, look at the conclusion. Does that conclusion have to be true in that world? Is it just a "maybe"? Or does it actually clash with the facts you just accepted? The answer to "by how much" the premises help the conclusion is what defines its position on the continuum.

There are five different zones on the Continuum of Support:

Must be False Claims

In this zone, the conclusion is a logical impossibility. If you accept the premises, the conclusion is fully against it and cannot be true at the same time.

Every participant in the marathon finished the race in under four hours. Sarah was a participant in the marathon. Sarah finished the marathon in four hours and fifteen minutes.

If "every" participant was under four hours, Sarah (who is a participant) cannot be at four hours and fifteen minutes. The conclusion directly contradicts the premises. The conclusion must be false on the basis of the premises.

Anti-Supported Claims

Here, the premises don't strictly forbid the conclusion, but they make it look like a very bad bet. It is inconsistent with the weight of the evidence.

An overwhelming majority of professional chefs prefer using gas stoves over electric ones because of the precise heat control. A randomly selected professional chef is going to prefer an electric stove for their kitchen.

While it’s possible the chef is in the minority group, the premises provide overwhelming evidence for the opposite. The conclusion is inconsistent with the probability established by the facts. The conclusion is anti-supported on the basis of the premises.

Unsupported Claims

This is the most common "trap" on the LSAT for some question types. The conclusion isn't necessarily contradicting anything, but the premises are completely silent about it. There is no logical thread connecting the two.

Most people who buy high-end cameras also purchase professional editing software. So most people who buy high-end cameras enjoy taking photos of urban architecture.

This is Unsupported. Knowing what software people buy tells us nothing about what they like to photograph. The premise and conclusion are both about cameras, but they are on different islands. There is no support relationship here.

Most Strongly Supported Claims

In this zone, we find strong, persuasive arguments. The conclusion isn't guaranteed by the laws of the universe, but it is the most reasonable inference given the facts.

In the last ten years, there has been a pattern of when the local tech company has missed its quarterly earnings projections, its stock price has dropped the following day. The company missed its earnings projections this morning. The company’s stock price will likely decrease tomorrow.

We have a decade of consistent patterns. While a freak market event could keep the price up, the premises make the conclusion highly likely. This is a most strongly supported conclusion to make.

Must be True Claims

In this zone, we find fully valid arguments. There is zero wiggle room. If the premises are true, the conclusion cannot be false.

To qualify for the senior discount, a customer must be at least 65 years old. Anyone who is at least 65 years old will utilize discounts they are offered. Arthur qualifies for the senior discount. Therefore, Arthur will utilize his senior discount.

The logic is airtight. The premise sets a rule, Arthur meets the rule, therefore the result is guaranteed . This is a Valid Inference and therefore the claim must be true.

Recap: Through the Continuum of Support you can now categorize any claim you encounter. The strength of an argument is a technical measure of the logical pressure the premises exert on the conclusion. By auditing this relationship on a continuum, we can categorize any claim from Must be False contradictions to Must be True guarantees. While valid arguments offer absolute certainty, most LSAT reasoning exists in the high-probability middle zones, where we must calculate the exact degree of support provided.
source_heading: Module 2, Lesson 19: The Continuum of Support
module_label: Module 2: , Lesson 19: The Continuum of Support

summary: Here is the final consolidation phase of Module 2. You have moved from a passive reader to a Logic Architect. You no longer see a block of text; you see a structural map of interconnected ideas. This lesson summarizes every tool in your arsenal, from identifying the "GPS" indicat
text_content:
Here is the final consolidation phase of Module 2. You have moved from a passive reader to a Logic Architect. You no longer see a block of text; you see a structural map of interconnected ideas. This lesson summarizes every tool in your arsenal, from identifying the "GPS" indicators to auditing the invisible "Gaps" in a stimulus.

1. The Skeleton: Premises & Conclusions

Every argument is a Support Relationship—a structural attempt to prove a specific point using foundational facts.

The Conclusion (The Destination): The primary claim the author wants you to believe. It is the “bottom line” that receives logical support from other claims but gives none back.

The Premise (The Route): The evidence or "Why" provided to justify the conclusion.

The GPS: Indicator Words

The LSAC often flags the anatomy of an argument using specific signposts.

Category

Indicator Examples

Function

Conclusion Flags

Thus, Therefore, Hence, Consequently, So, Clearly.

Signal the "Goal" or final interpretation.

Premise Flags

Because, Since, For, Given that, Due to.

Signal the "Evidence" or reasons why.

Pivot Flags

But, However, Yet.

Signal a "turn" from context to the author's actual claim.

Power Additives

Moreover, Furthermore, In addition, Likewise.

Act as transitions to link multiple premises together.

2. The Complex Map: Sub-Conclusions & Noise

Skyscraper arguments aren't just one story; they involve multiple levels of support and non-essential "Noise".

Minor Premises: A claim that instead of going directly to the main conclusion supports a “Sub-Conclusion” that then goes on to support the main conclusion. You can think of this as evidence for an existing premise.

Sub-Conclusions: A claim that wears two hats. It is supported by a "Minor Premise" but then turns around to support the "Main Conclusion".

Context (The Noise): Background information that sets the stage or describes what others believe. It is irrelevant to the logical proof and can often be ignored.

Concessions: When an author admits a counter-point ("Even though," "Despite") to anticipate or respond to an opponent's objection.

3. The Invisible Infrastructure: Assumptions & Gaps

The most vulnerable part of an argument is often what remains unsaid. An Assumption is the "Missing Link" or Gap between where the evidence ends and the conclusion begins.

The Spectrum of Assumption Reasonableness:

Reasonable: Gaps that are widely accepted facts or logical necessities.

Unreasonable: Subjective, extreme, or biased leaps (e.g., trusting self-reported feelings as objective facts).

The "Wrecking" Test: If you negate an assumption and the conclusion no longer follows, you have found a load-bearing link.

The Principle of Charity: We must interpret arguments in a charitable light, not automatically assume falsity of assumptions unless we have a reason to do so.

4. The Continuum of Support

"Strong" is a measure of the logical pressure premises exert on a conclusion. We can audit this relationship on a 5-zone spectrum.

summary: This Active Drill will be PT105.S4.Q7. This is a main conclusion question in regards to a married couple joining the same corporation.
text_content:
This Active Drill will be PT105.S4.Q7. This is a main conclusion question in regards to a married couple joining the same corporation.

The Question:

Willett: Lopez and Simmons, a married couple, have both been offered jobs at Evritech Corporation. Because Evritech has a rule against hiring more than one member of the same family, Lopez and Simmons have decided to reveal their marriage to Evritech. Their decision is foolish, however, since it will mean that one of them will have a job offer withdrawn. After all, they could easily keep their marriage secret initially and, if they want, later claim to have married after they were hired: Evritech has no policy of terminating one of two employees who marry each other.

The main conclusion of Willett's argument is that

Lopez and Simmons should not both have applied for jobs at Evritech Corporation

Evritech Corporation's rule against hiring more than one member of the same family is often not enforced

Lopez and Simmons would be unwise to reveal their marriage to Evritech Corporation without already having started to work there

Evritech Corporation should be willing to employ two members of the same family if it is willing to retain two of its employees who marry each other

Evritech Corporation is not likely to discover the marital status of Lopez and Simmons if they do not volunteer the information

We will input this question as the active drill. I will record a video explanation for this later but we will also have this written explanation for it.

In this active drill, we put your architectural skills to the test with a Main Conclusion question. You needed to filter for the core, identify the "Pivot," and use the "Why" Test to find the argument's true destination.

Stimulus Analysis

Let's deconstruct the argument by identifying the role of each sentence.

Lopez and Simmons, a married couple, have both been offered jobs at Evritech Corporation. Because Evritech has a rule against hiring more than one member of the same family, Lopez and Simmons have decided to reveal their marriage to Evritech.

This entire first sentence is Context. It sets the stage by identifying the characters, the company’s anti-nepotism rule, and the couple’s current plan. The author isn't trying to prove these facts; they are simply "setting the table" so the argument has a place to sit.

Notice the word "because" in this sentence. In a shorter stimulus, this might flag a conclusion is coming up (e.g., "They decided to reveal their marriage because of the rule"). However, in this longer passage, this entire situation is just a factual setup. We are looking for what the author thinks about this situation, not just a description of what the couple is doing.

Their decision is foolish, however, since it will mean that one of them will have a job offer withdrawn.

This is the Logical Heart of the stimulus where the author's voice finally enters the room. The word "however" is your Pivot Flag, signaling that the author is turning away from the background data and toward their actual point. The author's primary claim is that the couple's decision is "foolish." This is a Value Judgment, which is one of the three primary missions of an LSAT conclusion. The second half of the sentence, introduced by the indicator "since," provides the immediate Premise or "Why": it will result in a lost job offer.

After all, they could easily keep their marriage secret initially and, if they want, later claim to have married after they were hired: Evritech has no policy of terminating one of two employees who marry each other.

The phrase "After all" is a strong "GPS" marker signaling that the sentences following it are premises provided to justify the "Goal". This sentence outlines a viable alternative strategy, proving that the couple's original plan is unnecessary. It provides two foundational facts: (1) secrecy is easy, and (2) the company does not fire employees who marry post-hiring. These facts support the author's interpretation that the current plan is a mistake. This is a Regular Premise that receives no support from other sentences but provides support to the conclusion that the decision is foolish.

Characterize Your Answer

Before looking at the answers, let’s confirm our conclusion by applying the "Why" Test . This diagnostic tool helps us distinguish between the "Pillars" (premises) and the "Weight" (conclusion).

If we ask the author, "Why should I believe that Lopez and Simmons' decision is foolish?," the author has multiple answers: "Because it leads to a job loss, and they have an easy alternative that avoids this because the company doesn't fire people who marry after being hired." This flow makes perfect structural sense.

However, if we try to flip it to another claim—"Why should I believe the company doesn't fire people who marry after being hired?"—it would be totally gibberish to say, "Because the couple's current decision is foolish".

Meaning, it is clear the first two sentences are just context introducing the facts of the situation. The third sentence includes two claims, starting with the conclusion and then a supporting premise after the work “since.” The last sentence is additional premises supporting the main conclusion that their decision is foolish. Therefore, we are hunting for an answer choice that accurately restates the author’s primary judgment: that revealing the marriage at this stage is a strategic mistake.

Answer Choice Analysis

First let me tell you a golden rule for Main Conclusion questions: if a claim is not explicitly mentioned in the stimulus, it cannot be the conclusion. Many students fail because they try to be too smart and start inferring what the author might think or what would be "fair" in the real world.

On the LSAT, the Main Conclusion is the "Goal" that actually appears on the page. If an answer choice introduces a brand new idea, policy, or recommendation that wasn't in the original 60 words, you must delete it immediately. It is no longer in the running.

A) Lopez and Simmons should not both have applied for jobs at Evritech Corporation.

This answer choice is a classic "Out of Scope" distractor. This idea is never mentioned in the stimulus. It attempts to broaden the argument's focus to an action that occurred much earlier in the timeline of our story, essentially positing a value judgement about whether that should have happened when the author never said anything as such. The argument takes the job offers as a starting factual foundation—a "given" that sets the stage for the actual drama. Remember, the author is laser-focused on a very specific moment: the decision to reveal the marriage given that they have already been offered the jobs.

By suggesting that the mistake was "applying" in the first place, this choice introduces a recommendation that is never addressed in the text. In the lab, this goes beyond the logical heart of the stimulus. Since the author does not provide a single premise to support the idea that they shouldn't have applied, this choice must be disqualified instantly.

B) Evritech Corporation's rule against hiring more than one member of the same family is often not enforced.

This answer choice fails because it describes a frequency of enforcement that is never mentioned or even suggested by Willett. The stimulus tells us that a rule exists, but it provides zero data on how often that rule is ignored or applied to others.

In fact, this choice is an inference of the text that actually contradicts the internal logic of the argument. The author’s entire reason for calling the decision "foolish" is the belief that the rule will be enforced, resulting in a withdrawn job offer. If the rule were "often not enforced," then revealing the marriage wouldn't necessarily be foolish because they might get to keep both jobs anyway.

On the Continuum of Support, this statement would be classified as Anti-Supported in the context of Willett’s reasoning. It undermines the very premise that the author uses to support the conclusion. Therefore, it cannot be the conclusion itself.

If you choose this, you are bringing in outside assumptions that the author never authorized and should reiterate to stick to only what’s mentioned. Because there is no factual evidence in the premises to support a claim about "enforcement frequency," this choice cannot possibly be the bottom line for the argument's logic. It is an unsupported island.

C) Lopez and Simmons would be unwise to reveal their marriage to Evritech Corporation without already having started to work there.

This is the CORRECT answer. It is a highly accurate, professional paraphrase of the author’s primary Value Judgment. Calling a decision "foolish" is functionally equivalent to calling it "unwise." Furthermore, the phrase "without already having started to work there" perfectly captures the author's proposed alternative of waiting until they are already hired.

This choice represents the mission for all the logical support in the stimulus and what we characterized as the answer in our prediction. It avoids the traps of being too broad or focusing on supporting premises, instead zeroing in on the author's final recommendation: discouraging the immediate disclosure of the marriage.

D) Evritech Corporation should be willing to employ two members of the same family if it is willing to retain two of its employees who marry each other.

This choice is a trap that is trying to capitalize on our pre-existing notions about what ought to be. It presents a moral or principled argument about what the company should do (fairness/consistency), rather than what the couple should do (strategy). This sentiment is not mentioned by the author. While the author uses the inconsistency in Evritech's rules as a factual premise to show that the couple can get away with their secret, the author's "Goal" is not to reform corporate policy.

The LSAT frequently uses these distractors to catch students who get emotionally involved in the story because it sounds like a reasonable "lesson" to learn from the story. However, your job is not to find a reasonable lesson; it is to find the author's specific conclusion. Since Willett never makes a recommendation to the corporation, this choice has no home in the stimulus and should be disregarded immediately.

E) Evritech Corporation is not likely to discover the marital status of Lopez and Simmons if they do not volunteer the information.

This choice is the Premise Trap that is the only other answer choice that is actually found in the stimulus. While this statement is implied by the author's claim that they could "easily keep their marriage secret," it is simply a premise, not the conclusion. The author mentions the likelihood of secrecy remaining intact as a reason why the couple's current plan is foolish. It was not the final point the author wants you to walk away believing.

In the lab, we identify the Main Conclusion by asking: "Why should I believe this?". If you ask, "Why should I believe the company won't discover the marriage?," the stimulus doesn't give you an answer. But if you ask, "Why is their decision foolish?," the facts about discovery and job loss provide the answer. Choice E is a supporting fact, but it is not the destination of the argument.

What can we learn from this question?

Reflect on the high-level skills required for this dissection:

Follow the Pivot: Always look for words like "however" or "but" to find where the author’s voice separates from the background noise.

Verify the role of each claim and characterize what the answer is before allowing the answer choices to trick you.

Apply the "Why" Test: By simulating the support relationship, you can prove which statement is the final destination and which are the supporting pillars.

Make sure to reflect on any issues you had with this question. Question what may have caused you to fall into the LSAT test writer’s trap and what specific areas you need to improve.

summary: This Active Drill will be PT146.S2.Q18. This is a tough argument part question dealing with an argument about pulsars in space.
text_content:
This Active Drill will be PT146.S2.Q18. This is a tough argument part question dealing with an argument about pulsars in space.

The Question:

The consensus among astronomers, based upon observations of the surfaces of pulsars, is that pulsars are spinning balls of neutrons compressed into a sphere some 10 kilometers in diameter with a mass roughly equal to that of our sun. However, their observed properties are also consistent with some pulsars actually being filled with quarks, the building blocks of neutrons. Because the core of a quark-filled pulsar, unlike a neutron-filled one, would have an overall positive charge, it would attract a layer of negatively charged particles that could support a crust of neutrons.

The statement that the core of a quark-filled pulsar would have an overall positive charge plays which one of the following roles in the argument above?

It helps explain how pulsars could have neutrons on their surface even if they were not entirely made up of neutrons.

It forms part of a challenge to the claim that some pulsars may be made up of quarks.

It helps explain why some pulsars would not be readily recognized as such by astronomers.

It presents a new finding that challenges the consensus view of the structure of pulsars.

It points out a problem with the view that pulsars have a mass roughly equal to that of our sun.

We will input this question as the active drill. I will record a video explanation for this later but we will also have this written explanation for it.

This question takes us into the deeper waters of the LSAT with an Argument Part question. For these questions, it is imperative that we don't just ask what the conclusion is of the stimulus; we audit the specific role every single sentence plays within the larger structure. This requires a high level of precision because you must determine if a statement is acting as a pillar of support, a background fact, or the main point itself.

Stimulus Analysis

Let's deconstruct the argument by identifying the role of each sentence.

The consensus among astronomers, based upon observations of the surfaces of pulsars, is that pulsars are spinning balls of neutrons compressed into a sphere some 10 kilometers in diameter with a mass roughly equal to that of our sun.

This entire first sentence is Context. It sets the stage by identifying the current "Consensus" or the established view in the scientific community. The author is not yet making an argument; they are simply reporting what others believe to provide a baseline for the discussion.

Notice the dense technical data regarding the diameter and the mass of the pulsars. In the lab, we recognize this as academic bloat. When you see confusing subject matter. Do not panic. I repeat, DO NOT PANIC. Everything on the LSAT is understandable and is made to be so, so read carefully (review the techniques found in our Grammar module). The author describes a few things in one sentence which we can separate to make it easy:

There is a consensus among astronomers about pulsars based on their observations.

They say pulsars are spinning balls of neutrons compressed into a sphere.

They are around 10 kilometers in diameter with a mass roughly equal to that of our sun.

Now we’ve understood what this is saying, we can identify this as part of the "Noise" of the stimulus; someone else’s perspective. What can we expect to come next? The author’s opinion responds to this consensus. When I first read this sentence, I thought to myself: So what? This is a tell-tale sign that this sentence was put there to set up the context before the author voices what he thinks about.

However, their observed properties are also consistent with some pulsars actually being filled with quarks, the building blocks of neutrons.

A key signal is that we encounter a major Pivot Flag: the word "However". This is the most critical structural marker in the passage. It tells us that the author is about to introduce an alternative theory or remark that challenges or complicates the consensus view mentioned in the first sentence. And as expected, the author voiced his response to the consensus in this claim following our “GPS” indicator word.

He posits that pulsars could be "quark-filled" rather than just "neutron-filled" based on the observable characteristics the astronomers used to validate their conclusion. If the argument now goes on to support why the observations are consistent with quark-filled pulsars, we’ll know this is the Bottom Line of the stimulus—the central claim that the author wants you to accept as a valid possibility. Every sentence following this pivot should be used to support this alternative theory.

Because the core of a quark-filled pulsar, unlike a neutron-filled one, would have an overall positive charge, it would attract a layer of negatively charged particles that could support a crust of neutrons.

This final sentence comprises two separate premises serving as the technical justification for the author's theory on why the pulsars could also be filled with quarks. It explains the mechanics of how a pulsar filled with quarks could still produce the observed properties that the astronomers see on the surface to come up with their neutron-filled theory.

This identifies the statement as two premises. The claim initiates a chain of support: the positive charge attracts negative particles, which then support a neutron crust. This mechanical chain is designed to prove the bottom line that the quark-filled theory is "consistent" with the fact that we see neutrons on the surface of pulsars.

Characterize Your Answer

So, as complicated as this theory first sounds, getting a rock-solid understanding of the stimulus sets us up perfectly to characterize what the chosen claim in the question stem is. Let’s remind ourselves on what claim they chose.

The claim mentioned in the question stem is “the core of a quark-filled pulsar would have an overall positive charge.” This claim is one of our premises that help show us why the observable traits of the surface being neutrons can still mean the pulsars are filled with quarks.

Knowing this, we are looking for an answer choice that accurately identifies this statement as a premise used to explain how a specific physical structure (the neutron crust) can exist on a quark-filled core.

By characterizing the statement as an explanatory premise that supports the author's alternative theory, we can bypass distractors that mislabel it as a conclusion or as an attack on the quark theory itself.

Answer Choice Analysis

We are hunting for the choice that correctly identifies our target statement as a premise used to reconcile the quark-filled core theory with the observed neutron surface.

A) It helps explain how pulsars could have neutrons on their surface even if they were not entirely made up of neutrons.

This is the CORRECT answer. It aligns perfectly with our characterization of the argument’s method of reasoning. The statement that the core has a positive charge is a premise that begins a mechanical explanation: the charge attracts particles, which in turn support a crust of neutrons. These were the “observable” traits the astronomers based their theory on.

By identifying the role as "helping explain" the surface composition, this choice recognizes the statement as a premise that bridges the gap between the author’s alternative theory (quark-filled) and the astronomers' surface observations (neutrons). It accurately maps how the author justifies that a pulsar doesn't have to be "neutrons all the way down" to look like a neutron star on the outside, thereby substantiating the conclusion.

B) It forms part of a challenge to the claim that some pulsars may be made up of quarks.

This choice describes the exact opposite of what is happening in the stimulus. The author is not using the positive charge to attack the quark theory; they are using it as the primary evidence to defend the quark theory as a potential hypothesis.

If you selected this, you likely confused the "Consensus" view with the author's voice. Be very weary about whose opinion is being voiced. The author is the one proposing the quark theory, and the positive charge is a helpful fact that makes their theory possible. It is a support pillar for the quarks, not a hammer used to smash them.

C) It helps explain why some pulsars would not be readily recognized as such by astronomers.

This is an unsupported description of the claim that introduces a concept never discussed in the text. Nowhere in the stimulus does the author suggest that pulsars are difficult to recognize or that astronomers are failing to identify them as pulsars. The entire debate is about internal composition, not external recognition.

To dismiss this answer, you should be crystal clear on what the chosen claim is supporting: the bottom line that quark-filled cores are "consistent" with observed properties. To say it explains a lack of recognition is to invent a new problem for the characters that isn't in the stimulus. As a Logic Architect, you must reject any choice that discusses recognizing pulsars when the text only discusses the makeup of them.

D) It presents a new finding that challenges the consensus view of the structure of pulsars.

This choice is a sophisticated trap that requires meticulous reading. While the author’s theory certainly challenges the "Consensus" view, this specific answer choice fails because it labels the target statement as a "new finding." In the BetterLSAT lab, we emphasize that if a detail is not explicitly mentioned, it does not exist for the purpose of the argument.

Nowhere does the stimulus suggest that the positive charge of a quark core is a "new" discovery or a recent finding. The author presents it as a theoretical property—a conditional "would have" statement—rather than a report on a recent breakthrough in a lab or observatory. On the LSAT, you cannot assume a finding is "new" simply because the argument feels modern or innovative.

Meticulousness reading means catching these small, descriptive qualifiers. If the author didn’t say the finding was "new," "recent," or "groundbreaking," then an answer choice claiming it is a "new finding" is making an unsupported description of our claim in question. This immediately disqualifies it from being the correct role. If you fell for this trap, make sure to always read every word of the answer choice you are choosing.

E) It points out a problem with the view that pulsars have a mass roughly equal to that of our sun.

This is Out of Scope noise that targets information we previously filtered out. In our initial audit, we categorized the pulsar's mass as part of the context—information about another group’s opinion used to introduce the topic but not relevant to the logical core of the argument.

The author’s argument about quark-filled cores and positive charges is entirely focused on electrical charge and observable surface outcomes, not on gravitational mass. The statement about positive charge has no logical connection to the pulsar's weight relative to the sun. By categorizing the mass as "Noise" early on, you can see that this choice is trying to trick you into connecting two unrelated parts of the stimulus. It fails because the author never uses the charge of the core to point out a "problem" with the mass. Be very clear on the crux of the issue to make sure not to fall into these types of trap choices.

What can we learn from this question?

Reflect on the high-level skills required for this dissection:

Categorize the Noise: By identifying the first sentence as “other people’s position”, we were able to instantly dismiss Choice E, which tried to make the "mass" relevant to an argument about the composition of pulsars.

Trust the GPS: The Pivot Flag ("However") and the Premise Indicator ("Because") allowed us to map exactly where the author's voice began and which facts were being used as support.

Read meticulously on this test, especially on the answer choices you choose. We rejected Choice D because the stimulus never explicitly called the information a "new finding." The LSAT doesn’t tolerate “almost”; it rewards strict adherence to the text.

Coming up…

Before you move into our final active drill of this module, brace yourself. The next challenge is a Necessary Assumption question, designed specifically to test your ability to identify those invisible assumptions that an argument absolutely requires to function.

Remember, a Necessary Assumption is a load-bearing claim that the argument's structure must rely on; if this assumption wasn’t made, the conclusion wouldn’t possibly be able to be substantiated from the premises. As you audit the choices, keep your most powerful diagnostic tool ready: the Negation Test. By looking at an answer choice is false, you can see if the logical bridge remains standing or if it is completely wrecked. If the argument collapses under the weight of that negation, you have successfully identified the required assumption.

Good luck, Logic Architect.
